proposals.title: Discriminator-Driven Anomaly Detection for Quality Assurance in Clinical Multi-Modal Imaging
proposals.motivation: Ensuring the quality and fidelity of clinical images (e.g., MRI, CT) is critical for accurate diagnosis and reliable AI model deployment. Current quality assurance (QA) methods are often rule-based or require extensive manual review, which is inefficient and may miss subtle, complex artifacts. Leveraging the powerful feature representations learned by deep generative models offers a promising, automated alternative for detecting anomalous or low-quality scans.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a discriminator network, trained within a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) framework on high-quality, multi-modal clinical images, can be repurposed as an effective, unsupervised anomaly detector that identifies low-quality or artifactual images based on their deviation from the learned distribution of 'real' data.
proposals.method: We will train a StyleGAN2-ADA or a similar state-of-the-art GAN on a curated dataset of high-quality, paired multi-modal clinical images (e.g., T1- and T2-weighted MRI). After training, we will isolate the discriminator (D). For a new image, D will output a 'realism' score; low scores will indicate potential artifacts or quality issues. We will integrate this with a self-supervised contrastive learning module (inspired by Gatidis's prior work) to learn robust feature representations of common artifacts, enhancing D's sensitivity. The system's performance will be validated against expert-annotated QA labels on held-out clinical data.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, automated QA tool for clinical imaging pipelines, reducing reliance on manual checks. It will demonstrate a new application for GAN discriminators beyond adversarial training, specifically for unsupervised anomaly detection in medical data. The integration with contrastive learning aims to create a more robust and interpretable system for flagging image quality issues.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in systematically inverting the conventional GAN role: using the *trained discriminator* as the primary QA tool, rather than the generator, and combining this with contrastive learning for artifact representation. This approach is distinct from using autoencoders or standard classifiers for anomaly detection.

proposals.title: Multi-Center Validation of Medical Generative Adversarial Networks: Assessing Robustness to Scanner and Institutional Heterogeneity
proposals.motivation: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) like MedGAN show promise for data augmentation and synthesis in medical imaging, but their real-world utility is limited by domain shift. Pre-2026, most validations use single-center data, failing to capture the variability in scanner protocols, manufacturers, and patient populations across institutions. This gap hinders the deployment of robust, generalizable AI models in clinical practice.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the performance and output fidelity of a MedGAN model will degrade significantly when applied to data from institutions and scanner types not seen during training, and that this degradation can be quantified and mitigated through specific adaptation strategies informed by domain shift analysis.
proposals.method: We will train a state-of-the-art MedGAN architecture (e.g., based on StyleGAN2-ADA) on a large, curated dataset from a single institution. Its generalization will be rigorously tested on held-out multi-center datasets (e.g., from The Cancer Imaging Archive) encompassing different MRI/CT scanner models and acquisition protocols. We will employ quantitative metrics (FID, SSIM) and a blinded expert evaluation to assess synthetic image quality and utility for downstream tasks. To analyze failure modes, we will integrate concepts from the researcher's prior work on avoiding shortcut learning, using mutual information minimization to probe whether the GAN exploits institution-specific artifacts rather than learning robust anatomical representations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, large-scale evaluation of MedGAN generalization across clinical domains, establishing concrete benchmarks for robustness. It will produce a publicly available framework for testing medical GANs on heterogeneous data and deliver actionable insights into the sources of domain shift that most critically impact generative model performance.
proposals.novelty: While multi-center studies exist for discriminative models, a focused, methodological investigation into the generalization limits of medical GANs across scanner heterogeneity is lacking. The novelty lies in coupling a large-scale validation with a diagnostic analysis rooted in shortcut-learning theory to explain performance degradation.

proposals.title: A Multi-Task Framework for Joint Medical Image Translation and Anatomical Segmentation via Mutual Information Regularization
proposals.motivation: In clinical practice, multi-modal imaging (e.g., T1/T2 MRI, CT) is crucial for diagnosis, but acquiring all modalities is often infeasible due to cost, time, or patient contraindications. Synthetic image generation via translation (e.g., CycleGAN) can fill this gap, but models risk learning superficial, non-anatomically faithful features (shortcuts), degrading downstream utility. A joint approach that enforces anatomical consistency during translation is needed to produce clinically reliable synthetic images.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dual-task network performing simultaneous image translation and anatomical segmentation, regularized by mutual information minimization between input and task-relevant features, will produce more anatomically plausible synthetic images and improve segmentation performance in low-annotation regimes compared to sequential or single-task models.
proposals.method: We will build upon a U-Net-based architecture with a shared encoder and two task-specific decoders for translation and segmentation. The framework will integrate: 1) a cycle-consistency loss for unpaired translation, 2) a segmentation loss on available annotations, and 3) a mutual information minimization penalty (inspired by Gatidis' prior work) applied to the shared encoder's features to prevent shortcut learning and force the model to capture robust anatomical representations. Self-supervised contrastive pre-training via random walks on unlabeled data will initialize the encoder.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified model for generating anatomically consistent synthetic medical images and accurate segmentation masks, directly addressing the shortcut-learning problem in image translation. It will demonstrate improved performance on downstream tasks like diagnosis or planning with synthetic data, particularly when annotated data is scarce.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit coupling of translation and segmentation tasks with mutual information regularization to combat shortcuts, moving beyond simple multi-task learning or sequential pipelines. This integrates Gatidis' expertise in avoiding shortcut-learning, contrastive pre-training, and LLM-assisted clinical analysis into a novel imaging framework.

proposals.title: Direct Clinical Parameter Inference from Medical Imaging via an Invertible Shape-to-Image Model
proposals.motivation: Current clinical workflows often rely on an intermediate, explicit segmentation of anatomical structures from imaging data (e.g., cardiac ventricles) to derive quantitative parameters (e.g., ejection fraction). This two-step process is time-consuming, prone to error propagation, and requires extensive manual annotation. A method that directly infers clinical parameters from raw imaging data, bypassing explicit shape reconstruction, would streamline analysis, reduce costs, and improve accessibility in resource-limited settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deep learning model, trained with a combination of contrastive self-supervision and mutual information constraints, can learn a latent representation that directly encodes clinically relevant parameters from imaging data without explicitly modeling intermediate anatomical shapes.
proposals.method: We will develop an encoder-decoder architecture where the encoder maps an input image (e.g., a cardiac MRI slice) to a low-dimensional latent vector. This latent space will be regularized using a contrastive learning objective, inspired by random-walk methods, to ensure it captures robust, patient-specific features. Crucially, we will apply mutual information minimization between the latent code and potential shortcut features (e.g., imaging artifacts) to force the model to learn the true underlying pathophysiology. The decoder will be trained to reconstruct the input image, ensuring the latent space is invertible and information-rich, while a parallel, lightweight regression head will be trained to predict target clinical parameters directly from the latent code.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel end-to-end framework for direct clinical parameter estimation that eliminates the need for explicit segmentation, 2) a robust latent representation learning strategy combining contrastive learning and mutual information minimization to avoid shortcut solutions, and 3) empirical validation on a cardiac MRI dataset, demonstrating performance comparable to or better than traditional two-step methods while being more efficient.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly inverting the conventional modeling paradigm—instead of deriving parameters from a reconstructed shape, we learn a shape-informed latent representation from which parameters are directly extracted, enforced by invertibility and anti-shortcut constraints to ensure clinical validity.

proposals.title: MedShapeBench: A Multi-Modal Benchmark for Standardized Evaluation of 3D Anatomical Shape Completion and Reconstruction
proposals.motivation: The field of medical image reconstruction and shape completion lacks standardized, multi-modal benchmarks, hindering fair comparison and reproducible progress. Current methods are often validated on disparate, small-scale datasets, making it difficult to assess generalization and robustness. A comprehensive benchmark built on a large, diverse dataset is crucial for driving innovation in clinical applications like surgical planning and implant design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that creating a standardized, multi-task benchmark for 3D anatomical shape completion and reconstruction will reveal systematic performance gaps of current methods and establish clear, reproducible evaluation protocols that accelerate methodological development.
proposals.method: We will curate a large-scale dataset from existing public and institutional repositories of paired medical imaging (e.g., CT, MRI) and 3D anatomical meshes. The benchmark will define multiple tasks, including partial-to-complete shape reconstruction and image-to-shape generation. We will implement baseline models using state-of-the-art architectures like 3D UNets and implicit neural representations (e.g., Occupancy Networks). Evaluation will be based on a suite of geometric (Chamfer Distance, F-score) and clinical metrics, with analysis of failure modes and shortcut-learning risks inspired by mutual information minimization principles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a publicly available, rigorously designed benchmark suite for 3D medical shape tasks. It will establish standardized evaluation protocols and strong, reproducible baselines, enabling meaningful comparison of future methods. The analysis will highlight specific challenges like domain shift and data efficiency, guiding targeted research.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing benchmarks focused on 2D segmentation or classification, MedShapeBench specifically targets 3D geometric reconstruction tasks with a multi-modal, clinical focus. Its design explicitly incorporates lessons from shortcut-learning research to create challenging evaluation scenarios that test for genuine anatomical understanding rather than dataset artifacts.

proposals.title: Conditional Diffusion Models for Patient-Specific Anatomical Shape Generation and Augmentation in Medical Imaging
proposals.motivation: Generating realistic and diverse anatomical shapes is crucial for data augmentation in medical image analysis, especially for rare pathologies where annotated data is scarce. Current generative models like GANs often suffer from mode collapse and training instability, limiting their ability to capture the full spectrum of anatomical variability. Diffusion models, which have shown remarkable success in natural image generation, offer a promising alternative for creating high-quality, diverse 3D shape representations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a conditional diffusion model, trained on a dataset of segmented anatomical structures, can generate more diverse and realistic synthetic shapes than state-of-the-art GANs, and that using these shapes for data augmentation will improve the robustness of downstream segmentation models, particularly in low-annotation regimes.
proposals.method: We will develop a 3D denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM) operating on point cloud or implicit neural representation (e.g., Signed Distance Function) embeddings of anatomical shapes from public datasets (e.g., ShapeNet-Med). The model will be conditioned on clinical descriptors (e.g., organ label, disease stage, patient demographics) using cross-attention layers. To mitigate shortcut learning and ensure the model learns meaningful anatomical features, we will incorporate a mutual information minimization penalty between the generated shape and the conditioning vector during training, building on Gatidis's prior work. The generated shapes will be validated via quantitative metrics (e.g., Frechet Point Cloud Distance) and used to augment training data for a contrastive learning-based segmentation network.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for controllable medical shape generation, directly addressing data scarcity in medical imaging. It will demonstrate the utility of diffusion models over GANs for this domain and offer a practical tool for creating synthetic populations for computational anatomy studies and robust AI training.
proposals.novelty: While diffusion models have been applied to 2D medical image synthesis, their application to 3D anatomical shape generation conditioned on clinical metadata is novel. The integration of mutual information minimization to prevent condition-shape shortcuts represents a unique methodological fusion of Gatidis's expertise in robust deep learning and generative modeling.

proposals.title: Few-Shot Anatomical Shape Classification via Prototypical Networks and Geometric Feature Embeddings
proposals.motivation: In medical imaging, acquiring large, annotated datasets of 3D anatomical shapes (e.g., from CT or MRI) for rare pathologies or anatomical variants is a major bottleneck. This limits the development of robust diagnostic and prognostic models. Few-shot learning (FSL), successfully demonstrated on general 3D shape datasets like ShapeNet, offers a promising path to leverage limited labeled medical shape data effectively.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting few-shot classification frameworks, specifically prototypical networks, to the medical shape domain by incorporating domain-specific geometric feature embeddings will enable accurate classification of anatomical structures and pathologies with only a handful of examples per class.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Prototypical Network architecture, a leading FSL method, for 3D point clouds or mesh data representing anatomical structures. The core innovation is the design of a hybrid embedding network: a standard PointNet++ backbone will be augmented with a parallel branch that computes hand-crafted geometric descriptors (e.g., shape diameter, curvature histograms). These features will be fused and used to compute class prototypes in the embedding space. Training will employ episodic sampling on a base dataset of common anatomical shapes, with evaluation on novel, rare shape classes using a limited support set.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, data-efficient framework for medical shape analysis, directly applicable to rare disease classification and anatomical phenotyping. It will also contribute a benchmark for few-shot learning on medical shape data, bridging the gap between general computer vision and clinical 3D imaging.
proposals.novelty: While FSL is established for natural images and ShapeNet objects, its application to anatomical shapes is novel. The integration of learned and explicit geometric features within the FSL paradigm specifically addresses the unique, topology-preserving nature of anatomical structures, moving beyond generic shape classification.

proposals.title: Cross-Domain Super-Resolution for Validating 1.5T Cardiac MRI Tissue Classification: A Hybrid Learning Approach
proposals.motivation: Cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR) at 3T offers superior signal-to-noise ratio for tissue characterization but is less accessible and more costly than ubiquitous 1.5T systems. This creates a clinical and research disparity. Validating 1.5T CMR for precise tissue mapping (e.g., fibrosis, edema) could democratize advanced cardiac phenotyping, improving patient access and enabling larger-scale studies.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid model, trained on paired 1.5T and 3T CMR scans, can generate synthetic high-quality images from standard 1.5T inputs, enabling tissue classification performance comparable to native 3T analysis, thereby challenging the necessity of 3T for accurate tissue mapping.
proposals.method: We will adapt a self-supervised contrastive learning framework, inspired by recent work on limited annotations, to learn a shared feature space between paired 1.5T and 3T scans. A super-resolution network (e.g., an Enhanced Deep Residual Network, EDSR) will be trained using this representation, with a mutual information minimization loss to prevent shortcut learning and force genuine feature enhancement. The enhanced 1.5T images will be fed into a hybrid classification algorithm (e.g., a vision transformer or CNN) to produce tissue maps, which will be rigorously validated against ground-truth 3T segmentations using Dice scores and clinical metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, validated method to enhance 1.5T CMR data, potentially establishing it as a sufficient platform for clinical tissue characterization. It contributes a cross-domain translational framework, adapting techniques from remote sensing to medical imaging, and could significantly impact clinical guidelines and resource allocation for cardiac MRI.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic cross-domain adaptation of a 'data fusion' paradigm from satellite imaging to a critical clinical MRI problem, combined with the integration of pre-2026 advanced training strategies (contrastive learning, mutual information minimization) specifically to avoid shortcuts and ensure robust feature enhancement for validation.

proposals.title: Modeling the Cardiac Phenotypic Niche: A Maximum Entropy Framework for Predicting Clinical Trajectories from Hybrid PET/MRI Data
proposals.motivation: In cardiology, hybrid PET/MRI provides rich multi-parametric data on fibrosis, inflammation, and metabolism, yet prognostication often relies on single biomarkers, failing to capture complex tissue-environment interactions. This limits personalized risk prediction for outcomes like heart failure hospitalization. Ecological niche modeling (ENM) excels at predicting species survival from complex environmental variables, offering a powerful but unexplored analogy for integrating multi-source imaging data into a unified prognostic model.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying maximum entropy (MaxEnt) and ensemble modeling principles from ENM to hybrid cardiac imaging data will identify a high-risk 'phenotypic niche'—a specific combination and interaction of imaging biomarkers—that robustly predicts adverse long-term clinical trajectories.
proposals.method: We will curate a longitudinal cohort with cardiac PET/MRI and clinical outcomes. Coregistered imaging features (e.g., T1/T2 maps, ECV, FDG uptake) will serve as environmental layers. Using the MaxEnt algorithm, we will model the 'niche' associated with a positive outcome (e.g., event-free survival). To enhance robustness and avoid shortcut learning from confounding features, we will integrate mutual information minimization techniques during feature encoding. The model will be validated for time-to-event prediction against traditional Cox models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, systems-based prognostic framework for cardiology that moves beyond univariate associations. It will deliver an interpretable model identifying critical multi-parametric biomarker thresholds for clinical risk stratification, directly informing patient management. The successful cross-domain application of ENM establishes a new paradigm for analyzing complex, multi-modal medical data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the conceptual translation of ecological niche modeling to medical imaging prognosis, a direct analogy not previously explored. Methodologically, it combines pre-2026 ENM algorithms (MaxEnt) with contemporary deep learning regularization techniques (mutual information minimization) specifically for hybrid imaging data fusion.

proposals.title: Physiology-Informed Radiomics for Cardiac PET/CMR: A Multi-Modal, Adversarial Feature Discovery Framework for Explainable Biomarkers of Myocardial Disease
proposals.motivation: Current radiomics in cardiology often repurposes generic, oncology-derived features, lacking a direct link to underlying myocardial pathophysiology like inflammation or fibrosis. This limits biological interpretability and clinical adoption. A first-principles framework for cardiovascular imaging is needed to discover robust, explainable biomarkers from multi-parametric PET and CMR data that reflect specific disease mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: A physiology-informed radiomic framework, using adversarial and self-supervised learning to minimize non-biological imaging confounders, can discover novel, multi-modal texture features that are intrinsically linked to specific myocardial disease substrates and offer superior prognostic value compared to conventional features.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-stage pipeline. First, we will generate a large bank of candidate texture features from co-registered PET (e.g., FDG, perfusion) and CMR (T1, T2, ECV) maps. A mutual information minimization network, inspired by Gatidis's prior work on avoiding shortcuts, will filter features that are invariant to scanner/protocol variations. A contrastive learning module, using random walks on patient similarity graphs built from clinical data, will group features by shared pathophysiology. Finally, an adversarial classifier will validate discovered features against gold-standard histology or clinical outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first open-source, cardiovascular-specific radiomic feature bank with documented physiological correlates. It will provide a validated, multi-modal biomarker discovery pipeline that prioritizes technical robustness and biological explainability, moving cardiac radiomics beyond black-box pattern recognition towards mechanistically grounded tools for risk stratification.
proposals.novelty: Unlike generic radiomic studies, this framework is built from first principles for cardiac physiology, integrating adversarial robustness and self-supervised learning specifically to isolate biologically relevant signal from multi-modal imaging confounders, ensuring discovered features are explainable and disease-specific.

proposals.title: Cross-Modal Radiomics for Subtle Pattern Discovery in Cardiac Sarcoidosis: A Multi-Sequence CMR and PET Fusion Study
proposals.motivation: Cardiac sarcoidosis (CS) diagnosis and risk stratification remain challenging due to subtle, heterogeneous myocardial involvement not fully captured by standard clinical imaging reads. Radiomics, which extracts high-dimensional quantitative features from medical images, has proven powerful in oncology for uncovering prognostically relevant patterns but is under-explored in inflammatory cardiomyopathies. Applying this methodology to CS could reveal novel imaging biomarkers for early detection and personalized management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a radiomics framework applied to multi-parametric cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR) maps and 18F-FDG PET images will identify subtle, complementary texture patterns predictive of clinical outcomes in CS, surpassing conventional imaging metrics.
proposals.method: We will extract a comprehensive panel of first-order, shape, and higher-order texture features (e.g., GLCM, GLRLM, GLSZM) from segmented myocardium on late gadolinium enhancement, T1/T2 mapping, and PET images. To avoid shortcut-learning from imaging artifacts, we will integrate Mutual Information Minimization (MIM) during deep feature extraction. A multi-stage feature selection pipeline will reduce dimensionality before training an ensemble model (e.g., XGBoost) to predict major adverse cardiac events. Model interpretability will be enhanced using SHAP values.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first cross-modal radiomics signature for CS, providing a quantitative tool for improved patient phenotyping. It will contribute a validated, interpretable model that links multi-parametric imaging textures to clinical outcomes, potentially guiding therapy decisions. The methodological integration of MIM addresses a critical robustness gap in medical image analysis.
proposals.novelty: While radiomics is established in oncology, its systematic application to the multi-modal imaging landscape of CS is novel. The fusion of deep radiomic features from CMR and PET, regularized by mutual information minimization to ensure biological relevance, represents a significant methodological advance beyond standard single-modality analyses.

proposals.title: Anatomically Grounded, Evidence-Attributing Fusion Network for Interpretable Classification of Cardiac Sarcoidosis Activity from PET/CMR
proposals.motivation: Accurately distinguishing active from chronic cardiac sarcoidosis using multi-modal PET/CMR imaging is critical for treatment decisions but remains challenging. Current deep learning classifiers are often 'black boxes,' lacking interpretable evidence, which hinders clinical trust and adoption. An automated system that provides a transparent, spatially explicit audit trail of its decision-making would bridge the gap between AI performance and clinical utility.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural network with a dedicated, anatomically constrained attribution module can achieve high classification accuracy for cardiac sarcoidosis activity while generating spatially aligned, multi-modal evidence maps that are both clinically interpretable and faithful to the model's reasoning.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stream encoder for fused 18F-FDG PET and CMR (T2-weighted, LGE) data. An attribution module will generate 3D saliency maps using gradient-based methods (e.g., Grad-CAM++) or a trainable attention mechanism. This module will be constrained by a pre-trained cardiac segmentation network (e.g., nnU-Net) to ensure attributions are confined to anatomically plausible myocardial regions. A lightweight, fine-tuned clinical LLM (e.g., BioBERT or ClinicalBERT) will synthesize the attribution maps and image features into a concise, structured text report citing evidence type, location, and intensity.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel multi-modal classification framework that provides intrinsic, anatomically plausible explanations, moving beyond post-hoc interpretability. It will also demonstrate a prototype for generating machine-generated, evidence-backed imaging reports, directly addressing the need for transparent AI in diagnostic workflows.
proposals.novelty: The integration of an attribution module directly constrained by anatomical segmentations to produce structurally grounded explanations is novel. Furthermore, the end-to-end synthesis of multi-modal attribution maps into a succinct evidence report represents a significant advance over standard class activation maps.

proposals.title: Adversarially Robust, Multi-Tracer PET/MRI with Bayesian Model Selection for Specific Quantification of Myocardial Inflammation
proposals.motivation: Non-invasive specific quantification of myocardial inflammation remains a critical unmet need in cardiology. Current FDG-PET protocols suffer from low specificity due to confounding physiological myocardial glucose uptake, while single-tracer kinetic modeling is prone to bias from model misspecification. A method to disentangle specific inflammatory signals from general metabolism and provide robust, voxel-wise quantification would significantly improve diagnostic accuracy for conditions like myocarditis and cardiac sarcoidosis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dual-tracer (FDG + CXCR4-targeted) dynamic PET protocol, analyzed with a voxel-wise Bayesian model selection framework and integrated with multi-parametric MRI via an adversarially trained classifier, will yield a specific inflammation activity score with significantly higher diagnostic accuracy than standard FDG-PET methods.
proposals.method: We will implement a dual-tracer dynamic PET acquisition on a hybrid PET/MRI scanner. For each voxel, Bayesian model selection (e.g., using Bayes factors or WAIC) will be applied to a library of candidate kinetic models to identify the most appropriate one, reducing misspecification bias. The resulting parametric maps (e.g., Ki for FDG, BPND for CXCR4 tracer) will be coregistered with cardiac MRI (T2 mapping, LGE). A multi-modal classifier (e.g., a 3D CNN) will be trained on these fused features to output a diagnosis and activity score. Inspired by recent work on avoiding shortcut learning, we will employ mutual information minimization or adversarial training during classifier development to ensure it relies on robust, physiologically relevant features from both modalities, not spurious correlations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, fully integrated pipeline for specific myocardial inflammation imaging, providing clinicians with a quantitative, multi-parametric activity score. It will advance multi-tracer kinetic modeling by introducing robust Bayesian model selection at the voxel level. The adversarially trained multi-modal classifier sets a new standard for robust feature integration in hybrid imaging.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic combination of voxel-wise Bayesian kinetic model selection for dual-tracer PET and the use of adversarial robustness techniques (mutual information minimization) to build a trustworthy multi-modal diagnostic classifier, moving beyond simple feature concatenation.

proposals.title: A Closed-Loop, Adaptive MRI Framework for Rapid Triage via Real-Time Sequence Optimization and Decision-Making
proposals.motivation: Current rapid MRI protocols for triage are rigid, risking missed diagnoses when unexpected pathologies fall outside a fixed protocol's scope. This is a critical bottleneck in emergency and high-throughput settings where both speed and diagnostic accuracy are paramount. An intelligent system that dynamically personalizes the scan based on initial findings could maximize diagnostic yield without sacrificing throughput.
proposals.hypothesis: A closed-loop MRI system, using a deep learning model to analyze an initial ultra-fast multi-contrast scout and then optimize subsequent sequence selection in real-time, will achieve higher diagnostic sensitivity for a broad range of pathologies compared to a static truncated protocol, while maintaining equivalent scan time.
proposals.method: The system begins with a sub-2-minute multi-contrast scout (e.g., T1, T2, DWI). A segmentation model, trained with self-supervised contrastive learning and random walks to handle limited annotations, analyzes this scout for anatomical landmarks and suspected abnormalities. A decision module, formulated as a reinforcement learning agent or an LLM fine-tuned on clinical guidelines (inspired by Gatidis's work on clinical text), then selects and triggers the next optimal sequence from a predefined library. To prevent the model from relying on scanner-specific 'shortcuts', we will incorporate mutual information minimization techniques during training.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, clinically translatable framework for adaptive MRI acquisition, moving beyond static protocol compression. It contributes a new benchmark for evaluating diagnostic yield per unit time in rapid imaging. The integrated use of limited-annotation segmentation, shortcut-robust training, and clinical language-informed decision logic represents a significant methodological advance.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on accelerating fixed sequences or reconstructing undersampled data, this proposal introduces real-time, content-driven *acquisition* optimization within a closed clinical feedback loop. The novel integration of a decision-making agent, informed by both image analysis and clinical reasoning models, to physically control the MRI scanner sequence execution is a non-obvious and transformative step.

proposals.title: Federated Harmonization and Adaptive Learning for Robust Biobank-Scale Phenotype Classification
proposals.motivation: Large-scale population biobanks are crucial for discovering imaging biomarkers of disease. However, applying classifiers across multiple cohorts is hindered by technical variation from different scanners and protocols, which introduces spurious biases and reduces generalizability. Current centralized harmonization methods often fail to preserve privacy and do not adapt to local population characteristics, limiting their utility for robust, large-scale analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a federated learning framework, initialized with a model trained on harmonized data using ComBat-GAM, will yield a phenotype classifier that is more robust and generalizable across heterogeneous biobanks than models trained on centralized or non-harmonized data, by minimizing site-specific technical confounds while adapting to genuine biological variation.
proposals.method: We will first harmonize multi-site imaging data using ComBat-GAM to remove scanner-induced variation. An initial hybrid classifier (e.g., a CNN for imaging features combined with a tabular data module) will be pre-trained on this harmonized dataset. This model will then be deployed in a federated learning system (using frameworks like PySyft or Flower) where it is iteratively refined on local cohort data without sharing raw data. To prevent shortcut learning on residual confounds, we will integrate a mutual information minimization penalty during federated updates, as inspired by Gatidis's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, privacy-preserving pipeline for deploying imaging classifiers across biobanks, directly addressing the reproducibility crisis in medical imaging AI. It will deliver a methodological framework for federated harmonization and a benchmark for robust, multi-cohort phenotype classification.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the sequential integration of state-of-the-art harmonization (ComBat-GAM) with a federated adaptive learning loop, explicitly designed to suppress technical confounds while preserving privacy—a combination not yet explored for biobank-scale phenotype analysis. The use of mutual information minimization within the federated context to prevent shortcut learning is also a novel adaptation.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Multimodal Framework for Population-Level Phenotyping and Risk Stratification in Large-Scale Imaging Biobanks
proposals.motivation: Large-scale imaging biobanks like the UK Biobank hold immense potential for discovering novel imaging biomarkers of disease. However, extracting robust, generalizable, and clinically relevant features from these vast datasets remains a major challenge. Current methods often rely on single-modality analysis or supervised learning with limited annotations, failing to fully leverage the rich, multi-modal data (imaging, clinical text, outcomes) available.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid, self-supervised framework that integrates contrastive learning on medical images with large language model (LLM) processing of associated clinical text will yield superior, shortcut-resistant representations for population-scale disease classification and outcome prediction compared to unimodal or fully supervised approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stream architecture. For the imaging stream, we will apply a self-supervised contrastive learning method, specifically a random-walk-based strategy as in Gatidis' prior work, to learn robust image features from unlabeled scans in the biobank. For the clinical text stream (e.g., radiology reports, patient summaries), we will adapt a pre-trained clinical LLM for information extraction and summarization. A fusion module, trained with mutual information minimization to avoid shortcut learning, will integrate these representations. The final model will be fine-tuned on curated outcome data (e.g., disease incidence) for retrospective analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, scalable pipeline for multimodal phenotyping in population imaging. It will demonstrate the utility of combining self-supervised vision and language models for biomedical discovery. The resulting framework and discovered imaging-text correlates will serve as a resource for hypothesis generation and risk stratification.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific integration of contrastive image learning and clinical LLMs at a population biobank scale, explicitly engineered to mitigate shortcut learning—a critical concern for generalizable biomarker discovery. This moves beyond proof-of-concept single-disease studies to a systematic, cohort-wide analytical approach.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Imaging and LLM Framework for Standardized Metabolic Signatures in Inflammatory Cardiomyopathy
proposals.motivation: Differentiating cardiac sarcoidosis from other inflammatory cardiomyopathies (e.g., myocarditis) using FDG-PET is critically limited by high physiological myocardial glucose uptake, leading to false positives and diagnostic uncertainty. Current qualitative 'hot spot' assessment lacks reproducibility and quantitative rigor, delaying specific therapy. A standardized diagnostic paradigm is urgently needed to improve accuracy and enable earlier intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: That a rigorous, patient-specific dietary preparation protocol will suppress physiological myocardial FDG uptake sufficiently to unmask distinct, quantifiable metabolic and perfusion patterns, enabling a hybrid imaging and language model framework to generate objective, probabilistic diagnoses.
proposals.method: We will implement a strict, supervised low-carbohydrate, high-fat dietary protocol prior to hybrid 18F-FDG PET/MRI. Image analysis will use self-supervised contrastive learning (inspired by Gatidis' prior work) on limited annotations to segment pathological regions from standardized PET and late gadolinium enhancement MRI. Extracted multi-parametric features will train a machine learning classifier. Concurrently, structured patient information and imaging reports will be processed by a clinically adapted large language model (LLM) to generate standardized summaries. A final multimodal fusion model will integrate imaging signatures and LLM-derived clinical context for a probabilistic diagnosis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, quantitative imaging biomarker system for inflammatory cardiomyopathies, moving beyond subjective assessment. It will provide a validated dietary-imaging protocol for metabolic standardization and deliver a diagnostic AI tool that fuses imaging data with clinical context, directly improving diagnostic accuracy and reproducibility in clinical practice.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a rigorous metabolic standardization protocol with a multimodal AI framework that combines self-supervised image analysis and adapted LLMs for clinical text represents a significant advance over conventional, non-quantitative FDG-PET interpretation.

proposals.title: Adversarial Multi-Modal Fusion for Real-Time, Functionally-Guided Myocardial Targeting in Electrophysiology Procedures
proposals.motivation: Current precision targeting for cardiac ablation or biopsy relies on static pre-procedural PET/MRI, which cannot capture the dynamic functional state of the myocardium during the procedure. This static-to-dynamic mismatch can lead to suboptimal lesion placement and procedural failure. A system that integrates live intra-procedural data streams to define a dynamic functional target is critically needed to improve outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deep learning model trained with mutual information minimization to avoid spurious correlations can dynamically and robustly fuse live electrophysiological mapping, intracardiac echo strain, and prior metabolic imaging to identify target regions with a higher predictive value for pathology than any single modality or static pre-procedural imaging alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-branch neural network to process high-density electroanatomic maps (EAM), ICE-derived strain sequences, and pre-procedural PET/MRI embeddings. Inspired by Gatidis's prior work, we will employ a mutual information minimization objective between branches during training to prevent shortcut learning and force the model to discover genuine cross-modal relationships. A contrastive learning framework, using random walks on a patient graph constructed from multi-modal features, will leverage limited annotated data. The fused output will be a real-time, dynamically updating probability map highlighting target regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first validated ML framework for real-time, functionally integrated targeting in interventional cardiology, moving beyond static image overlay. It will provide a novel clinical decision support tool that directly addresses the dynamic nature of cardiac pathophysiology. The methodological contribution includes a new adversarial fusion technique for highly heterogeneous, temporally misaligned clinical data streams.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the adversarial, shortcut-resistant fusion of a static prior scan with live, high-frequency intra-procedural data streams to create a dynamic target model, a significant departure from current hybrid imaging and simple registration approaches.

proposals.title: Volumetric Inpainting for Medical Image Completion via 3D Patch-Based Adversarial Learning with Mutual Information Regularization
proposals.motivation: In clinical practice, 3D medical scans (e.g., CT, MRI) are often corrupted by artifacts or missing data due to patient motion, metal implants, or limited field-of-view, degrading diagnostic quality and downstream analysis. Existing 2D inpainting methods fail to capture the crucial 3D anatomical context and continuity, leading to inconsistent and unrealistic repairs across slices. A robust 3D volumetric inpainting framework is essential for generating plausible, structurally coherent tissue for improved quantitative analysis and visual assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a 3D generative adversarial network (GAN), trained with a self-supervised patch-based objective and regularized by mutual information minimization, can synthesize anatomically consistent and high-fidelity content for missing regions in volumetric medical images, outperforming slice-by-slice 2D approaches.
proposals.method: We will extend the 2D patch-based framework to 3D by developing a 3D Wasserstein GAN with a gradient penalty. The generator will be a 3D U-Net, processing full volumes. Training will use a self-supervised strategy where random 3D cuboids are masked and the network learns to reconstruct them. Crucially, to avoid shortcut solutions and ensure inpainted content is semantically meaningful, we will incorporate a mutual information minimization loss between the inpainted region and the immediate surrounding context, encouraging reliance on broader anatomical priors. This builds directly on Gatidis's prior work on avoiding shortcut learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dedicated framework for coherent 3D medical image inpainting, moving beyond 2D approximations. It will deliver a validated tool for artifact correction and data completion in volumetric scans. The integration of mutual information regularization offers a principled approach to improve generalization and anatomical plausibility in generative image tasks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the holistic 3D treatment of inpainting as a volumetric completion problem, combined with an information-theoretic regularization strategy specifically designed to prevent the model from learning trivial, context-dependent shortcuts, ensuring synthesis is driven by true anatomical understanding.

proposals.title: EfficientGAN: A Lightweight and Fast Generative Adversarial Network for Real-Time Medical Image Synthesis and Enhancement
proposals.motivation: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) have shown significant promise for medical image synthesis, data augmentation, and artifact reduction. However, their high computational cost and slow inference times, due to complex architectures and large parameter counts, preclude their use in real-time clinical workflows such as intraoperative imaging or rapid diagnostic support. Optimizing these models for speed without sacrificing fidelity is a critical, unsolved challenge for clinical translation.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating architectural innovations from lightweight computer vision with training techniques that minimize shortcut learning, we can develop a GAN framework that achieves a 10x reduction in inference time while maintaining diagnostic image quality comparable to state-of-the-art, heavier models.
proposals.method: We will design a generator based on a MobileNetV3-inspired inverted residual block architecture and a multi-scale patch discriminator. To ensure efficiency, we will employ neural architecture search (NAS) to optimize the network depth and width. Training will integrate mutual information minimization (inspired by Gatidis's prior work) between the input and generated output to prevent shortcut solutions and encourage robust feature learning, alongside a contrastive loss on intermediate feature maps to stabilize training with limited annotations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a publicly available, efficient GAN model specifically validated for medical imaging tasks, enabling new real-time applications. It will also provide a methodological framework for compressing and accelerating generative models in medicine while maintaining robustness against deceptive shortcuts.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the co-design of a NAS-optimized, mobile-friendly generator with a training regimen that explicitly combats shortcut learning, a combination not explored in prior medical GAN efficiency research. This moves beyond simple model pruning by holistically addressing architecture, training stability, and generalization.

proposals.title: Vision Transformer-Based Inpainting for Context-Aware Reconstruction of Pathological Medical Images
proposals.motivation: Accurate inpainting of missing or artifact-corrupted regions in medical images (e.g., MRI, CT) is crucial for downstream tasks like diagnosis and treatment planning. Existing convolutional neural network (CNN) methods often struggle to capture the long-range anatomical dependencies and structural consistency required for realistic reconstructions, especially around complex pathologies. This limitation can introduce biases in automated analysis and compromise clinical utility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a vision transformer (ViT)-based inpainting model, leveraging self-attention mechanisms, will outperform state-of-the-art CNN-based methods by better capturing the global contextual relationships in medical images, leading to more anatomically plausible and structurally consistent reconstructions of missing regions.
proposals.method: We will adapt a transformer-based inpainting architecture, such as a variant of the Masked Autoencoder (MAE), specifically for 2D/3D medical images. The model will be pre-trained in a self-supervised manner on large-scale public datasets (e.g., RadImageNet) by randomly masking patches and learning to reconstruct them. To mitigate shortcut learning and ensure the model utilizes true contextual information, we will incorporate a mutual information minimization penalty between the inpainted content and the immediate boundary of the masked region, drawing on Gatidis's prior work. Fine-tuning and evaluation will be conducted on curated datasets with simulated and real artifacts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, publicly available benchmark for transformer-based medical image inpainting, demonstrating the advantage of global context modeling. It will contribute a method that improves the fidelity of image restoration for clinical use cases like metal artifact reduction and lesion removal for data augmentation. The integration of mutual information minimization offers a principled approach to avoid local shortcut solutions.
proposals.novelty: While transformers are established in natural image inpainting, their application to medical image inpainting is nascent, and a systematic investigation of their ability to model long-range anatomical context for pathology reconstruction is lacking. The novel integration of mutual information minimization from Gatidis's previous research to enforce meaningful long-range learning in this specific task is a key innovation.

proposals.title: Patient-Specific Anatomical Adaptation for Medical Image Inpainting via Test-Time Meta-Learning
proposals.motivation: Deep learning models for medical image inpainting (e.g., artifact correction, lesion removal) are typically trained on population data and may underperform on patients with atypical anatomy or rare pathologies. This mismatch can degrade reconstruction quality, impacting downstream clinical decisions. Test-time adaptation offers a promising solution by fine-tuning models to individual patient data at inference, but effective and efficient methods for this in medical imaging are underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learning framework, pre-trained to rapidly adapt, combined with a test-time training objective based on mutual information minimization, will enable a single inpainting model to personalize its parameters to a specific patient's anatomy, significantly outperforming a static model.
proposals.method: We will build upon a U-Net-based inpainting architecture. A Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) framework will pre-train the model on a diverse dataset so its initial parameters are sensitive to patient-specific fine-tuning. During test-time training for a new patient, we will adapt the model using a self-supervised loss on the patient's own masked and unmasked image regions. Crucially, to avoid shortcut learning on local artifacts, we will incorporate a mutual information minimization penalty between the inpainted output and the input's artifact patterns, inspired by Gatidis's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical framework for patient-adaptive medical image reconstruction, moving beyond one-size-fits-all models. It will provide empirical evidence on the efficacy of test-time meta-learning in clinical imaging tasks and demonstrate how mutual information regularization can prevent overfitting to patient-specific noise during adaptation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic integration of meta-learning for efficient test-time adaptation with a mutual information-based objective specifically designed to preserve generalizable anatomical features during patient-specific fine-tuning, a combination not yet explored for medical inpainting.

proposals.title: Mutual Information-Guided Multi-Scale Contrastive Learning for Ultra-High-Resolution Whole-Slide Image Segmentation with Minimal Annotations
proposals.motivation: Whole-slide pathology images (WSIs) are gigapixel-scale, posing a fundamental computational and modeling challenge for deep learning. Current methods often rely on patch-based processing, which can lose global context and require extensive pixel-wise annotations that are costly and time-consuming for pathologists. Adapting efficient, annotation-light frameworks to this ultra-high-resolution domain is crucial for scalable computational pathology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a framework combining self-supervised contrastive pre-training on a multi-scale pyramid of image tiles with a mutual information minimization objective to prevent shortcut learning on local features will enable accurate segmentation of WSIs using only sparse, slide-level annotations.
proposals.method: We will adapt a random-walk contrastive learning framework, inspired by Gatidis's prior work, to operate on a multi-scale WSI pyramid. A vision transformer (ViT) encoder will be pre-trained via self-supervision using contrastive loss across scales. For downstream segmentation with limited scribble or point annotations, we will integrate a mutual information minimization penalty, as explored in Gatidis's previous research, between the encoder's intermediate feature maps and the input to discourage shortcut solutions based on local texture or artifacts. Inference will use a sliding-window approach with overlapping context.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, end-to-end trainable pipeline for ultra-high-resolution medical image analysis that significantly reduces annotation burden while maintaining segmentation accuracy. It will provide empirical evidence on large public WSI datasets (e.g., TCGA, Camelyon) that mutual information regularization improves robustness in multi-scale self-supervised learning. The code and pre-trained models will be released to the community.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic integration of multi-scale contrastive pre-training with explicit mutual information minimization for shortcut avoidance, specifically tailored for the extreme resolution and annotation constraints of computational pathology, a combination not yet explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Pseudo-CT Synthesis from Non-Attenuation-Corrected PET via Cycle-Consistent Adversarial Networks with Mutual Information Regularization
proposals.motivation: In PET/CT imaging, attenuation correction (AC) is crucial for quantitative accuracy but requires a CT scan, increasing radiation dose and acquisition time. Non-attenuation-corrected (NAC) PET images are routinely acquired but lack anatomical detail. A method to generate pseudo-CTs directly from NAC-PET without paired training data would enable retrospective AC for PET-only studies and reduce the need for paired acquisitions, enhancing patient safety and scanner flexibility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deep learning model trained with cycle-consistency and mutual information minimization can learn the underlying anatomical mapping from NAC-PET to CT using unpaired datasets, generating synthetic CTs accurate enough for clinical attenuation correction.
proposals.method: We will employ a CycleGAN architecture to learn the mapping between unpaired NAC-PET and CT domains. To prevent shortcut learning where the generator ignores the NAC-PET input, we will integrate a mutual information minimization loss between the input NAC-PET and the generated pseudo-CT, forcing the model to utilize the structural information in the PET data. The network will be trained using a self-supervised contrastive loss on image patches to improve segmentation of anatomical boundaries from the noisy PET signal, enhancing output fidelity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, clinically practical framework for generating attenuation maps from existing NAC-PET data, eliminating the strict requirement for paired CTs. It will advance unsupervised medical image translation by demonstrating how to avoid modality collapse in highly asymmetric domain transfers. The resulting method could be integrated into PET reconstruction pipelines to improve quantitative accuracy in studies where CT was not acquired.
proposals.novelty: While CycleGANs have been used for unpaired medical image translation, their direct application to the NAC-PET to CT task is novel. The key innovation is the explicit integration of mutual information minimization to prevent the generator from learning a trivial, input-ignoring solution—a critical risk given the low structural correlation between NAC-PET and CT compared to other modality pairs like MR-CT.

proposals.title: Weakly-Supervised Anatomical Segmentation via Ultra-Low-Dose CT Guidance and Mutual Information Regularization
proposals.motivation: Diagnostic-quality CT scans expose patients to ionizing radiation, limiting their use for large-scale, repeated data collection needed for training deep learning models. Ultra-low-dose CT (ULDCT) drastically reduces radiation but introduces severe noise and artifacts, making direct diagnostic use challenging. This creates a bottleneck for developing robust medical imaging AI, especially for segmentation tasks where pixel-level annotations are scarce and expensive.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the anatomical structure preserved in ULDCT scans, despite low signal-to-noise ratio, provides sufficient weak supervision to train a segmentation network for diagnostic CT, when combined with regularization techniques that prevent the network from learning shortcut solutions based on ULDCT-specific artifacts.
proposals.method: We will train a segmentation network (e.g., a nnU-Net variant) on paired diagnostic CT (target) and ULDCT (guidance) volumes. The network will receive the diagnostic CT as input, but its training will be guided by a multi-task loss. A primary segmentation loss will be computed against pseudo-labels generated from the ULDCT scan using a pre-trained, frozen denoising autoencoder. Crucially, we will incorporate a Mutual Information Minimization (MIM) penalty between the network's internal feature maps and the ULDCT input to discourage shortcut learning of ULDCT-specific noise patterns, forcing the model to rely on diagnostically relevant features. Self-supervised contrastive pre-training on a large corpus of ULDCT scans will be used to initialize robust feature extractors.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel pipeline for leveraging ultra-low-dose, non-diagnostic imaging for supervising high-quality image analysis models, significantly relaxing data acquisition constraints. It will contribute a new methodological framework combining weak supervision with information-theoretic regularization for robust learning from noisy guidance. The resulting models will demonstrate that diagnostic-quality annotations are not always necessary for achieving high segmentation performance, enabling safer and larger-scale data collection.
proposals.novelty: While ULDCT has been used for direct reconstruction or denoising, its explicit use as a weak supervisory signal for a separate diagnostic-quality task is novel. The integration of Mutual Information Minimization to avoid learning shortcut solutions from the ULDCT noise distribution represents a non-obvious and critical advancement over standard multi-modal or teacher-student approaches.

proposals.title: A Multi-Task Deep Learning Framework for Simultaneous Attenuation Correction and Pathological Lesion Segmentation in PET Imaging
proposals.motivation: In quantitative PET imaging, accurate attenuation correction (AC) is essential for reliable standardized uptake value (SUV) measurements, which are critical for lesion detection and characterization. Current deep learning methods for AC and lesion segmentation are typically developed as separate, sequential tasks, potentially leading to error propagation and suboptimal use of shared image features. A joint approach could improve the accuracy and clinical workflow efficiency of both tasks simultaneously.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-task network trained to jointly estimate attenuation maps and segment lesions from non-attenuation-corrected PET data will outperform sequential, single-task models by leveraging shared latent representations and enforcing mutual anatomical consistency between the tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-task encoder-decoder architecture with a shared encoder and two task-specific decoders. The network will be trained on paired non-AC PET, CT-based attenuation maps, and lesion segmentation masks. To prevent shortcut learning and encourage robust feature extraction, we will integrate a mutual information minimization penalty between the input and bottleneck features, inspired by Gatidis' prior work. A combined loss function will balance AC (mean squared error) and segmentation (Dice loss) objectives.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified, end-to-end framework that improves the quantitative accuracy of PET AC while directly outputting clinically relevant lesion segmentations. It will demonstrate the benefits of multi-task learning for coupled image reconstruction and analysis tasks in nuclear medicine, potentially leading to more reliable and automated diagnostic pipelines.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit joint modeling of the traditionally sequential problems of AC and lesion segmentation, and the application of mutual information minimization to prevent the network from relying on trivial, task-specific shortcuts in the input data.

proposals.title: Causal Anatomical Generative Models: Preserving Pathophysiological Relationships in Synthetic Medical Image Generation
proposals.motivation: Current generative models for medical imaging, such as GANs and diffusion models, often learn spurious correlations from data, producing anatomically plausible but causally incoherent images. This limits their utility for creating robust training data or for counterfactual 'what-if' analysis in clinical decision support. A method that explicitly models the causal relationships between anatomical structures and pathological manifestations is needed to generate semantically valid and trustworthy synthetic images.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating causal graphical models with deep generative networks, we can learn disentangled latent representations where interventions on causal factors (e.g., disease severity) lead to semantically correct changes in generated images, preserving the underlying pathophysiological relationships.
proposals.method: We will develop a Causal-VAE or a Causal Diffusion Model framework. First, a structural causal model (SCM) will encode domain knowledge (e.g., 'ventricular enlargement causes sulcal widening') as a directed acyclic graph over latent variables. We will use differentiable causal discovery techniques, like NOTEARS, to refine this graph from data. The generative model will be trained with a loss combining reconstruction, a causal regularization term enforcing independence between exogenous noise variables (inspired by Gatidis's prior work on mutual information minimization), and a counterfactual consistency loss. Training will utilize limited annotated datasets via self-supervised contrastive pre-training on image patches.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for causally-aware medical image synthesis, enabling the generation of counterfactual images for explainability and robust data augmentation. It will contribute a benchmark for evaluating the causal fidelity of generative models in medicine, moving beyond pixel-level metrics to semantic and clinical validity.
proposals.novelty: While causal representation learning is emerging, its application to *generation* in medical imaging is nascent. The key novelty is the explicit enforcement of causal relationships *during* the generative process, not just in post-hoc analysis, by combining SCMs with state-of-the-art generative architectures and mutual information constraints to avoid shortcut learning.

proposals.title: Beyond the Contour: Training Segmentation Models with Clinical Report Embeddings as Weak Supervision for Ambiguous Lesions
proposals.motivation: In medical imaging, manual segmentation is often treated as the sole gold standard, but for many pathologies (e.g., diffuse infiltrates, complex tumors), precise pixel-wise annotation is inherently ambiguous, subjective, and labor-intensive. This reliance creates a bottleneck for model development and may misrepresent the true clinical task, which is often to characterize a finding's presence and relevance rather than its exact boundary. Leveraging readily available clinical reports as an alternative source of supervision could enable training on larger, more representative datasets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dense semantic embeddings of free-text clinical reports, generated by a domain-adapted Large Language Model (LLM), can serve as a robust supervisory signal to train a segmentation network, achieving performance comparable to models trained on manual segmentations for clinically ambiguous regions.
proposals.method: We will develop a dual-encoder framework. A clinical text encoder (based on a pre-trained clinical LLM like ClinicalBERT or BioMedLM) will process paired radiology reports to produce dense embeddings. A visual encoder (a CNN like a U-Net) will process the corresponding image. Instead of pixel-wise loss, we will use a contrastive learning objective (e.g., InfoNCE) to align image region features with the global text embedding, encouraging the model to segment regions semantically described in the report. We will integrate mutual information minimization techniques from Gatidis's prior work to prevent the visual encoder from exploiting spurious, non-causal correlations in the imaging data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for training segmentation models without pixel-level annotations for ambiguous cases, directly aligning model outputs with clinical language. It will challenge the dogma of segmentation as the only valid ground truth and demonstrate a practical method to leverage unstructured clinical data at scale for supervision.
proposals.novelty: While weak supervision exists, using dense clinical report embeddings via LLMs as the primary training signal for segmentation is novel. The approach uniquely bridges the semantic gap between descriptive language and pixel-level predictions, moving beyond simple classification labels to richer, context-aware supervision.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Self-Supervised Learning for Treatment Response Assessment in PET/CT Using Multi-Timepoint Contrastive Alignment
proposals.motivation: In oncology, assessing treatment response via serial PET/CT imaging is critical but relies on expert qualitative analysis or semi-quantitative metrics like SUV change, which may not capture complex spatio-temporal patterns. Automated, data-driven methods for longitudinal analysis are limited by the scarcity of pixel-level annotations for follow-up scans. This creates a need for methods that can learn robust representations of tumor progression from unlabeled longitudinal imaging sequences.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a self-supervised learning framework, which explicitly models the temporal consistency and anatomical correspondence between serial PET/CT scans, can learn superior representations for downstream tasks like response classification and progression segmentation, outperforming models trained on single timepoints or with standard supervised learning.
proposals.method: We will develop a dual-stream 3D convolutional network, processing pairs of baseline and follow-up PET/CT scans. Inspired by recent contrastive learning and mutual information minimization techniques, we will employ a novel loss function combining: 1) a longitudinal contrastive loss (adapted from random-walk ideas) that pulls latent representations of the same anatomical region (via deformable registration) closer across time while pushing apart dissimilar regions, and 2) a mutual information minimization penalty between the learned features and easily identifiable imaging shortcuts (e.g., scanner-specific artifacts or body contour changes) to ensure the model focuses on pathologically relevant changes. The pre-trained encoder will be fine-tuned for response classification (e.g., PERCIST categories) and segmentation of progressing tumor sub-regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel self-supervised framework for leveraging unlabeled longitudinal medical imaging data, directly applicable to clinical trials and treatment monitoring. It will yield a publicly available benchmark dataset of curated serial PET/CT pairs with response labels. The method aims to set a new state-of-the-art for automated, interpretable treatment response assessment.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning has been applied to single medical images, its adaptation to enforce temporal consistency in *registered* longitudinal scans is novel. Furthermore, integrating mutual information minimization specifically to avoid learning non-pathological temporal confounds (e.g., weight loss) in this longitudinal context is a unique contribution to robust representation learning.

proposals.title: Multi-Center Validation of a Self-Supervised Deep Learning Model for PET/CT Segmentation: Assessing Generalizability Across Institutions and Scanner Platforms
proposals.motivation: Deep learning models for medical image analysis often exhibit degraded performance when applied to data from institutions or scanner models not seen during training, a critical barrier to clinical deployment. This is particularly relevant for PET/CT, where variations in acquisition protocols, reconstruction algorithms, and scanner hardware introduce significant domain shifts. Rigorous external validation is therefore a prerequisite for establishing the real-world utility of any proposed model.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a segmentation model trained with self-supervised contrastive learning and mutual information minimization for domain-invariant feature extraction will maintain robust performance on external, multi-center PET/CT datasets, demonstrating superior generalizability compared to a standard supervised baseline.
proposals.method: We will validate a pre-existing model, developed using self-supervised contrastive learning with random walks for limited-annotation segmentation and trained with mutual information minimization to avoid scanner-specific shortcuts. The model will be evaluated on a curated, multi-center dataset of PET/CT scans from at least three independent institutions, using different scanner models (e.g., Siemens, GE, Philips). Performance metrics (Dice score, Hausdorff distance) will be compared against the original test set and a baseline U-Net. Statistical analysis will assess the significance of performance differences across centers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, multi-center benchmark for the model's generalizability, a step often missing in medical imaging research. It will yield concrete evidence on the effectiveness of combining self-supervised learning and shortcut-avoidance techniques for creating robust, scanner-agnostic models. The findings will inform best practices for developing clinically translatable AI tools in nuclear medicine.
proposals.novelty: While external validation is recognized as important, this study uniquely focuses on validating a model explicitly designed for generalizability via pre-2026 state-of-the-art techniques (contrastive self-supervision and mutual information minimization) against the specific domain shifts present in multi-center PET/CT data.

proposals.title: Masked Autoencoding for Self-Supervised Representation Learning in Multi-Modal PET/CT Volumes
proposals.motivation: PET/CT imaging is crucial for oncology, but large-scale annotation is expensive and time-consuming, limiting the development of robust deep learning models. Self-supervised learning (SSL) has shown remarkable success in natural images, but its adaptation to 3D, multi-modal medical volumes remains underexplored. Learning rich representations from abundant unlabeled PET/CT data could significantly improve downstream tasks like lesion segmentation and classification with minimal labeled data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a self-supervised pre-training strategy based on masked autoencoding, adapted for 3D multi-modal medical volumes, will learn transferable anatomical and functional representations that outperform training from scratch and other SSL methods on downstream clinical tasks with limited annotations.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture and Masked Autoencoder (MAE) framework for 3D PET/CT volumes. The model will receive concatenated, normalized PET and CT patches. A high proportion (e.g., 75%) of these patches will be randomly masked. The encoder, a standard ViT, processes only the visible patches, and a lightweight decoder reconstructs the missing PET and CT intensity values. Pre-training will use a large, unlabeled dataset. The learned representations will be evaluated by fine-tuning on limited annotated data for tasks like tumor segmentation, leveraging insights from Gatidis's prior work on contrastive learning and avoiding shortcuts.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a publicly available, pre-trained model for 3D PET/CT analysis, reducing annotation burden. It will provide a systematic evaluation of masked image modeling for multi-modal medical imaging and establish a new strong baseline for SSL in nuclear medicine. The project bridges the gap between cutting-edge computer vision SSL and practical clinical imaging challenges.
proposals.novelty: While MAE is established for 2D natural images, its application to 3D, dual-modality (PET/CT) medical data is novel. The key innovation is the joint, asymmetric reconstruction of functionally complementary signals (metabolic activity from PET and anatomy from CT) within a unified self-supervised framework, moving beyond single-modality or contrastive approaches.

proposals.title: A Graph Neural Network Framework for Multi-Lesion Profiling in Oncology: Integrating Spatial and Metabolic Relationships from PET/CT
proposals.motivation: In oncology, patients often present with multiple lesions, and their spatial distribution and metabolic heterogeneity are critical for staging, treatment planning, and prognosis. Current deep learning methods in medical imaging typically analyze lesions in isolation, failing to capture the systemic, inter-lesional relationships that may encode disease aggressiveness and metastatic patterns. Modeling these relationships as a patient-specific graph could provide a more holistic view of the disease.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a graph neural network (GNN) model, which explicitly encodes the spatial distances and metabolic (SUV) characteristics of multiple lesions as node and edge features, can learn representations that outperform isolated lesion analysis in predicting clinical outcomes such as progression-free survival.
proposals.method: We will construct patient graphs where nodes represent individual lesions, annotated from PET/CT scans. Node features will include radiomic features and SUV metrics. Edges will connect lesions based on 3D spatial proximity, with edge features encoding distance and potentially anatomical region relationships. We will employ a Graph Attention Network (GAT) or Message Passing Neural Network (MPNN) architecture to learn node embeddings, which will be pooled into a global graph representation. This representation will be used for downstream tasks, leveraging self-supervised contrastive learning (inspired by Gatidis's prior work) on the graph structure to improve learning with limited annotated cohorts.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel computational framework for multi-lesion analysis in oncology imaging, moving beyond single-lesion classification. It will provide a method to quantify inter-lesional relationships and demonstrate their prognostic value. The resulting model could serve as a tool for more precise patient stratification and a foundation for studying metastatic spread patterns.
proposals.novelty: While GNNs have been explored for single-organ segmentation or molecular networks, their application to model the explicit physical and functional relationships between distinct, distributed lesions within a patient's body is novel. This approach integrates the spatial graph of disease burden with rich node-level imaging phenotypes, a significant departure from standard ensemble or population-level analyses.

proposals.title: Weakly-Supervised Multi-Organ Localization via Image-Level Labels and Self-Supervised Text-Image Alignment
proposals.motivation: Manual segmentation for organ localization in medical imaging is a major bottleneck, limiting the scalability of AI models for large-scale, multi-institutional studies. Existing weakly-supervised methods often rely on single-modality image data or class activation maps, which can yield imprecise and unreliable spatial boundaries. A method that leverages readily available, non-pixel-level data sources is needed to reduce annotation burden while maintaining anatomical precision.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by combining self-supervised visual representation learning with a language-guided alignment module using radiology text reports, we can accurately localize multiple anatomical structures using only image-level labels, outperforming existing weakly-supervised baselines that use image data alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stream architecture. First, a vision encoder will be pre-trained using a contrastive random walk strategy on unlabeled 3D CT volumes to learn spatial consistency. Second, a text encoder, initialized from a clinical LLM (e.g., BioClinicalBERT), will process paired radiology reports to extract organ mentions. A cross-modal attention module will align image features with text-derived semantic embeddings, generating organ-specific attention maps as weak supervision for a final localization network.
