We will employ mutual information minimization between organ-specific features to prevent shortcut learning and ensure robust representations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for precise anatomical localization without voxel-level annotations, directly applicable to large retrospective datasets. It will demonstrate how clinical text, a ubiquitous but underutilized data source, can be effectively harnessed to guide visual model training, bridging the gap between language and imaging domains in radiology AI.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the integration of self-supervised image features with semantic embeddings from clinical LLMs within a single weakly-supervised localization framework, explicitly designed to avoid modality-specific shortcuts through mutual information regularization.

proposals.title: Robust Brain Structure Segmentation from Fast MRI Protocols via Self-Supervised Denoising and Multi-Modal Language-Guided Refinement
proposals.motivation: High-resolution T1-weighted MRI is the gold standard for automated brain segmentation but requires long acquisition times, limiting patient comfort and throughput. Fast, low-resolution, or motion-degraded clinical protocols are more common but produce images unsuitable for current segmentation models, creating a critical clinical-performance gap. This work addresses the need for robust segmentation tools that work directly on realistic, imperfect clinical data, improving accessibility and efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: A segmentation model trained with a self-supervised denoising objective and refined using multi-modal patient information (via adapted LLMs) can achieve performance on fast, low-quality MRI scans comparable to models trained on high-quality data, without requiring extensive manual annotations for the target protocol.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage pipeline. First, a segmentation network (e.g., nnU-Net) will be pre-trained using a self-supervised contrastive denoising task on unpaired low- and high-quality scans, inspired by random-walk contrastive learning. Second, we will incorporate a language-guided refinement module: an adapted clinical LLM will process available patient text (e.g., radiology reports, clinical notes) to generate contextual embeddings. These will condition the segmentation decoder via cross-attention, providing prior anatomical and pathological knowledge to resolve ambiguities in low-quality images.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a segmentation framework that operates effectively on fast, low-resolution MRI, directly translating to shorter scan times and broader clinical utility. It will demonstrate a novel integration of self-supervised image processing and language-model-derived clinical context to combat shortcut learning and improve model robustness. The method will be validated on a multi-protocol brain MRI dataset with expert manual segmentations.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely combines self-supervised denoising for protocol invariance with the semantic reasoning of clinical LLMs for context-aware refinement, moving beyond simple image-to-image translation. It directly challenges the necessity of high-quality input by leveraging the rich, often-underutilized textual patient context already present in clinical workflows.

proposals.title: Contrast-Agnostic Organ Segmentation in Multi-Sequence MRI via Modality-Invariant Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Clinical MRI protocols vary, producing images with different contrasts (e.g., T1, T2, Dixon) that alter tissue appearance. Current deep learning models typically require training on each specific contrast, limiting their robustness and generalizability in real-world clinical settings where protocol variations are common. A single model capable of segmenting organs from any common MRI contrast without retraining would significantly improve clinical workflow efficiency and model deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by learning a shared, contrast-invariant feature representation, a single neural network can accurately segment anatomical structures from multiple MRI sequences without requiring sequence-specific training or fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We will develop a dual-branch encoder architecture. The primary segmentation branch will be trained with a standard segmentation loss (e.g., Dice). A parallel, self-supervised auxiliary branch will enforce contrast invariance using a contrastive learning objective (e.g., SimCLR) on patches from different sequences of the same anatomical location. Inspired by Gatidis's prior work on avoiding shortcuts, we will incorporate a Mutual Information Minimization (MIM) penalty between the latent representations of different sequences and the segmentation output to prevent the model from relying on sequence-specific intensity shortcuts, forcing it to learn robust anatomical features.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a proof-of-concept, contrast-agnostic segmentation model for abdominal organs, validated on multi-sequence MRI datasets. It will provide a novel methodological framework for building robust medical imaging models that generalize across acquisition protocols, reducing the need for extensive, sequence-specific data collection and annotation.
proposals.novelty: While multi-contrast or multi-modal learning exists, our approach uniquely combines contrastive self-supervision with explicit mutual information minimization to *actively discourage* learning of contrast-specific features, moving beyond simple data fusion or augmentation towards learning fundamentally sequence-invariant anatomical representations.

proposals.title: Assessing Domain Robustness of Self-Supervised MRI Segmentation Models: A Multi-Protocol Abdominal Imaging Study
proposals.motivation: Deep learning models for medical image segmentation often fail to generalize beyond the specific scanner protocols and populations on which they are trained, limiting clinical utility. This is a critical barrier to deploying automated tools in diverse real-world settings. Evaluating model robustness across technical and demographic variations is essential for safe translation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that self-supervised contrastive learning models, particularly those trained with random-walk regularization and mutual information minimization, will demonstrate superior generalization performance across varying MRI protocols compared to standard supervised baselines, by learning more robust and protocol-invariant feature representations.
proposals.method: We will leverage a pre-trained model from Gatidis's prior work using self-supervised contrastive learning with random walks for limited-annotation segmentation. This model will be evaluated on held-out abdominal MRI datasets encompassing different scanner manufacturers (e.g., Siemens, GE, Philips), sequence parameters (e.g., T1-weighted, T2-weighted), and patient populations. Performance will be benchmarked against a supervised model trained with standard augmentation. We will employ mutual information minimization techniques, as referenced in Gatidis's prior work, as a potential fine-tuning strategy to explicitly discourage reliance on protocol-specific shortcuts, quantifying its impact on cross-domain accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, quantitative assessment of the cross-protocol robustness of state-of-the-art self-supervised segmentation methods in abdominal MRI. It will yield practical insights into the failure modes of models under domain shift and validate mitigation strategies like mutual information minimization. The findings will inform best practices for developing generalizable AI models in radiology.
proposals.novelty: While domain generalization is a known challenge, systematically evaluating the synergy of two advanced pre-2026 techniques—self-supervised contrastive learning with random walks and mutual information minimization for shortcut avoidance—specifically for abdominal MRI segmentation across real-world protocol variations is novel. This directly tests whether these methods' theoretical benefits translate to practical robustness.

proposals.title: Real-Time, Acquisition-Constrained MRI Segmentation via Lightweight Self-Supervised Networks and Information Bottleneck Optimization
proposals.motivation: Current deep learning models for medical image segmentation are typically applied post-acquisition, missing the opportunity to guide MRI scans in real-time. This limits the technologist's ability to adjust scan planes for optimal organ coverage or perform immediate quality assurance (QA), which can reduce scan failures and patient recall. Achieving this requires extreme inference speed under the strict computational and latency constraints of the ongoing acquisition process.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a segmentation model, explicitly optimized for speed via architectural constraints and trained with a self-supervised objective that minimizes shortcut learning, can deliver clinically acceptable organ segmentations from partial k-space data within the temporal constraints of real-time MRI acquisition.
proposals.method: We will develop a lightweight, encoder-decoder architecture (e.g., a heavily pruned UNet variant) combined with knowledge distillation from a larger, accurate teacher model. Training will employ a multi-task objective: 1) a self-supervised contrastive loss on features from augmented partial k-space reconstructions (inspired by random-walk methods) to learn robust representations from limited annotations, and 2) a mutual information minimization penalty (avoiding shortcut-learning) between early layer features and specific acquisition artifacts, forcing the network to rely on anatomical priors. Inference will be optimized for deployment on MRI scanner hardware.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated framework for real-time, acquisition-integrated segmentation, moving AI from a post-processing tool to an interactive scan guidance system. It will demonstrate a novel training paradigm that balances extreme speed with robustness to acquisition variability, directly addressing a key translational bottleneck in clinical MRI workflows.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the co-design of the model architecture and training objective specifically for the stringent latency and data-incompleteness (partial k-space) of real-time MRI, integrating speed optimization with principles from self-supervised learning and shortcut-learning avoidance to ensure reliability.

proposals.title: Self-Supervised Scale-Aware Representation Learning for Abdominal Organ Segmentation in Fetal and Pediatric MRI
proposals.motivation: Accurate organ segmentation in fetal and pediatric abdominal MRI is crucial for quantitative assessment of growth and development but remains challenging. Standard models trained on adult data fail due to dramatic differences in anatomical scale, organ proportions, and tissue contrast. There is a critical need for segmentation methods specifically adapted to the unique imaging characteristics of these developing populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a self-supervised contrastive learning framework, explicitly designed to learn scale-invariant and scale-sensitive features from unlabeled pediatric and fetal MRI, will enable robust abdominal organ segmentation with minimal manual annotations.
proposals.method: We will adapt a self-supervised contrastive learning strategy with random walks, as used in Gatidis's prior work, but modify the pretext task. We will generate multi-scale views of input volumes via anatomically plausible synthetic deformations and intensity augmentations mimicking pediatric/fetal contrasts. The contrastive loss will be combined with a mutual information minimization penalty between features and shortcut cues (e.g., absolute voxel size) to force the model to learn anatomy, not just scale. This pre-trained encoder will then be fine-tuned on a limited set of annotated fetal/pediatric abdominal MRIs using a standard segmentation decoder.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a publicly available, pre-trained model for pediatric/fetal abdominal MRI segmentation, reducing annotation burden. It will provide a methodological framework for adapting segmentation networks to populations with vastly different anatomical scales. The results will demonstrate the efficacy of scale-aware self-supervision in a clinically impactful, data-scarce domain.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly tackling the 'scale' as a confounding variable via mutual information minimization within a pediatric imaging context, moving beyond simple augmentation. This integrates Gatidis's expertise in avoiding shortcut learning and contrastive self-supervision into a new, clinically vital application area.

proposals.title: Task-Aware Image Quality Assessment via Mutual Information for Robust Medical Image Analysis
proposals.motivation: Traditional image quality metrics (e.g., SNR, SSIM) are generic and often correlate poorly with the performance of downstream diagnostic algorithms, leading to suboptimal image acquisition protocols and unreliable AI model deployment. A task-specific definition of quality is crucial for efficient clinical workflows and robust automated analysis, especially in resource-limited settings where perfect image quality is unattainable. This problem is central to translating AI from research to clinical practice.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a task-specific quality metric, defined as the mutual information between the acquired image and the target diagnostic label (e.g., tumor segmentation mask), can more accurately predict downstream algorithm performance than traditional metrics and can be used to guide adaptive image acquisition.
proposals.method: We will develop a deep learning framework where a quality prediction network is trained via a contrastive self-supervised objective on a large unlabeled medical image dataset to learn general representations. This network will then be fine-tuned using a mutual information minimization loss (inspired by Gatidis's prior work on avoiding shortcuts) to explicitly disentangle task-relevant from task-irrelevant features. The final quality score will be the predictive mutual information, estimated via a variational lower bound, between the image embeddings and labels from a pre-trained, frozen task-specific model (e.g., a segmenter).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, theoretically grounded framework for task-dependent image quality assessment (IQA) in medical imaging. It will deliver a practical tool for protocol optimization and failure prediction for AI diagnostic models. The results will challenge the prevailing use of task-agnostic quality metrics in clinical studies and AI validation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in formally defining quality via predictive mutual information and enforcing this through a shortcut-avoiding, disentangled representation learning approach, moving beyond correlation studies to a causal-inspired, learnable quality metric directly tied to algorithmic task performance.

proposals.title: Generalizing MRI Quality Assessment: A Self-Supervised Framework for Multi-Sequence and Multi-Anatomy Applications
proposals.motivation: Automated quality assessment (QA) of MRI scans is crucial for ensuring diagnostic reliability and optimizing workflows in clinical and research settings. Existing deep learning QA frameworks are often narrowly tailored to specific sequences (e.g., T1-weighted) and anatomies (e.g., brain), limiting their broad utility. A generalizable framework is needed to handle the diverse contrast mechanisms and anatomical structures encountered in routine multi-parametric MRI protocols.
proposals.hypothesis: A self-supervised quality assessment framework, initially developed for a specific MRI cohort, can be successfully generalized to a wide range of MRI sequences and anatomical regions by leveraging sequence-agnostic feature learning and domain adaptation techniques.
proposals.method: We will extend the core framework, likely based on self-supervised contrastive learning (e.g., SimCLR, BYOL) as indicated by Gatidis's prior work, to learn latent representations invariant to sequence type and anatomy. We will incorporate mutual information minimization techniques to prevent shortcut learning on scanner- or protocol-specific artifacts. The model will be trained and validated on a multi-institutional dataset comprising diverse sequences (T1w, T2w, FLAIR, DWI) and anatomies (brain, spine, abdomen, musculoskeletal). Transfer learning and few-shot adaptation will be evaluated for new, unseen sequence-anatomy pairs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified, data-efficient tool for automated QA across the MRI portfolio, reducing the need for bespoke model development for each new application. It will establish benchmarks for generalizable medical image QA and offer insights into learning transferable visual representations in the presence of high domain variability.
proposals.novelty: While prior QA models are sequence/anatomy-specific, this proposal systematically tackles generalization across both dimensions simultaneously. It uniquely combines self-supervised contrastive representation learning with mutual information-based regularization specifically to achieve this cross-domain robustness.

proposals.title: Generalizing Deep Learning for Medical Image Analysis: A Systematic Framework for Training on Diverse Imaging Artifacts
proposals.motivation: Deep learning models for medical image segmentation and reconstruction are highly sensitive to domain shifts, particularly from imaging artifacts not seen during training. This limits their clinical deployment, as artifacts from different scanner manufacturers, acquisition protocols, or patient motion can severely degrade performance. A robust solution requires models that generalize across the full spectrum of artifact types encountered in real-world clinical settings, a challenge not adequately addressed by current limited training datasets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematically expanding training data to include a wider, more representative variety of imaging artifacts—spanning sources like motion, metal, coil, and reconstruction artifacts—will force models to learn more generalizable and physiologically relevant features, thereby significantly improving their robustness and segmentation accuracy on unseen clinical data.
proposals.method: We will develop a data augmentation pipeline that synthesizes a diverse set of realistic artifacts based on physical models of MRI/CT acquisition. This will be integrated into a self-supervised contrastive learning framework, building on Gatidis's prior work, where positive pairs are generated by applying different artifact types to the same underlying image. To prevent the model from learning shortcuts based on artifact patterns, we will incorporate a mutual information minimization objective between the learned representations and a vector encoding the artifact type. The segmentation backbone will be a U-Net variant trained with this multi-task objective.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated methodological framework for creating artifact-robust medical image analysis models, directly addressing a key barrier to clinical adoption. It will contribute a new public dataset of synthetically artifact-corrupted medical images with detailed metadata, serving as a benchmark for robustness research. The findings will advance the understanding of how to force models to learn invariant anatomical representations.
proposals.novelty: While data augmentation is common, the systematic, physics-based generation of a comprehensive artifact spectrum for training, combined explicitly with mutual information minimization to discourage artifact-specific shortcut learning, represents a novel and principled approach to generalization. This moves beyond simple noise addition or adversarial training towards a more complete simulation of the clinical imaging environment.

proposals.title: Cross-Modal Semi-Supervised Learning for MRI Segmentation: Leveraging Unlabeled CT and Ultrasound via Contrastive Representation Alignment
proposals.motivation: High-quality MRI annotations are expensive and time-consuming to acquire, limiting the development of robust deep learning models for segmentation. Meanwhile, large repositories of unlabeled or sparsely labeled computed tomography (CT) and ultrasound (US) images exist, which share anatomical information with MRI but are acquired under different physical principles. Exploiting this multi-modal data pool through semi-supervised learning (SSL) could significantly reduce the labeling burden for MRI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model pre-trained with SSL objectives on abundant, unlabeled CT and US data can learn transferable anatomical representations that, when fine-tuned with minimal MRI labels, will achieve segmentation performance comparable to models trained on large, fully-labeled MRI datasets alone.
proposals.method: We will extend contrastive learning frameworks, like SimCLR or BYOL, to a cross-modal setting. A shared encoder will be trained on unpaired patches from MRI, CT, and US using a modality-invariant contrastive loss that maximizes agreement between anatomically similar regions across modalities while minimizing agreement with dissimilar ones. This pre-trained encoder will then be fine-tuned for a specific MRI segmentation task (e.g., organ segmentation) using a limited set of labeled MRI scans and a segmentation head. We will incorporate mutual information minimization techniques from Gatidis's prior work to prevent the model from relying on modality-specific shortcuts during pre-training.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for reducing MRI annotation needs by leveraging cheaper imaging modalities. It will deliver a publicly available, pre-trained cross-modal encoder for medical imaging and demonstrate a practical pathway for efficient model development in data-scarce MRI applications.
proposals.novelty: While SSL is established within single modalities, applying it *across* fundamentally different imaging modalities (MRI, CT, US) to bootstrap a label-scarce target modality is novel. The key innovation is the design of a modality-invariant contrastive pre-training that explicitly learns anatomical rather than signal-based features.

proposals.title: Edge-MRI: A Lightweight, Information-Preserving Model for Real-Time Image Quality Assessment on MRI Scanners
proposals.motivation: Real-time quality assessment of MRI scans at the scanner console can prevent patient recall and improve workflow efficiency. However, deploying large, expert-level deep learning models for this task is infeasible due to the limited computational resources and strict latency requirements of edge devices in clinical settings. A compact, fast model that maintains diagnostic accuracy is urgently needed to bring AI-assisted acquisition directly to the point of care.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying a mutual information-based distillation framework from a large, pre-trained medical image segmentation model, we can create a lightweight classifier that preserves critical diagnostic features for real-time quality assessment while avoiding shortcut learning that plagues compressed models.
proposals.method: We will employ a teacher-student distillation framework. The teacher will be a robust segmentation model (e.g., a U-Net variant) pre-trained with self-supervised contrastive learning on limited annotations. The student will be a highly efficient architecture like MobileNetV3 or a custom lightweight CNN. Crucially, distillation will be guided not only by output logits but also by Mutual Information Minimization (MIM) between intermediate student and teacher features to prevent the student from learning simplistic, non-generalizable shortcuts and to force it to retain semantically meaningful representations. The model will be trained and validated on a multi-site MRI dataset for the task of classifying scans as 'diagnostically acceptable' or 'needing repeat'.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, deployable model for edge-based MRI quality control, directly addressing the hardware constraints of scanner consoles. It will provide a novel methodology for creating compact medical imaging models that are robust to shortcut learning, ensuring their reliability in clinical practice. The resulting framework can be generalized to other real-time medical imaging tasks at the edge.
proposals.novelty: While model compression is well-studied, its application to real-time, on-scanner MRI assessment is novel. The key innovation is integrating Mutual Information Minimization—previously used to avoid shortcuts in full-scale models—into the distillation process specifically for a clinical edge-deployment scenario, ensuring the lightweight model's decisions are based on diagnostically relevant features.

proposals.title: Clinically Grounded Validation: Integrating Expert-Derived Metrics and LLM-Assisted Analysis for Medical Image Translation
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation of medical image translation models (e.g., MRI-to-CT synthesis) relies heavily on quantitative image similarity metrics (PSNR, SSIM) that often correlate poorly with clinical utility. This creates a critical gap where a model can produce visually realistic outputs that are misleading or diagnostically irrelevant for downstream tasks like radiotherapy planning or lesion detection. A clinically meaningful validation framework is urgently needed to bridge this gap and ensure translational impact.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that augmenting standard image similarity metrics with task-specific clinical evaluation criteria, derived from expert assessment and structured language model analysis, will provide a more robust and clinically relevant validation framework for medical image translation, revealing shortcomings invisible to pixel-based metrics.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-modal validation pipeline for a conditional GAN-based translation model. First, we will establish a set of clinical evaluation metrics (e.g., anatomical structure fidelity, absence of hallucinated features, suitability for dose calculation) through structured surveys with clinical experts. Second, we will leverage a fine-tuned clinical LLM (e.g., based on BioBERT or ClinicalBERT) to analyze paired radiology reports (from source and synthetic images) for consistency in key clinical findings. Finally, we will employ mutual information minimization techniques, inspired by Gatidis's prior work, during model training to discourage shortcut features that satisfy pixel metrics but fail clinical criteria.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, open-source validation framework that directly ties medical image translation performance to clinical endpoints. It will deliver new benchmarks for the field and demonstrate how LLMs can be repurposed for automated, scalable clinical evaluation. The resulting insights will guide the development of more robust and trustworthy translation models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic integration of expert-derived clinical metrics with LLM-based textual consistency analysis for validation, moving beyond purely image-based scores. Furthermore, proactively using mutual information minimization to avoid learning shortcuts against these new clinical metrics is a novel training paradigm.

proposals.title: MAML for Patient-Specific Adaptation: Meta-Learning for Fast Personalization of Multimodal Clinical LLMs
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) adapted for clinical text summarization show promise but struggle with patient-specific adaptation due to limited per-patient data and the risk of shortcut learning on spurious correlations. Current fine-tuning approaches are inefficient and can degrade model robustness when applied to individual patients or small, novel cohorts. A method for rapid, data-efficient personalization is needed to make these models truly useful at the point of care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learning framework, specifically Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML), can enable a base clinical LLM to rapidly adapt to new patients with minimal data, improving the quality and personalization of clinical text summaries while preserving general medical knowledge and avoiding harmful shortcut learning.
proposals.method: We will develop a multimodal meta-learning pipeline. A base encoder will process structured patient data and imaging features (extracted via a self-supervised contrastive network). This will be fused with clinical text embeddings from a pre-trained LLM. The core adaptation will use MAML, where each 'task' corresponds to a single patient's longitudinal data. The inner-loop optimization will perform a few gradient steps on a patient's private data to personalize the model, while the outer-loop meta-update will train the model's initial parameters to facilitate this fast adaptation. We will incorporate a mutual information minimization regularizer during meta-training to discourage shortcut learning on patient-identifying but clinically irrelevant features.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel meta-learning framework for patient-specific LLM adaptation in clinical settings, 2) empirical evidence on the data efficiency and summary quality gains of this approach compared to standard fine-tuning, and 3) a methodology that explicitly mitigates shortcut-learning risks during personalization, enhancing model reliability.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been explored for medical imaging, its application to personalize multimodal clinical LLMs for text generation is novel. The integration of mutual information minimization within the MAML loop to ensure robust personalization directly addresses a key weakness in adaptive models identified in prior work.

proposals.title: 3D-MIM: Mutual Information Minimization for Robust, Annotation-Efficient Segmentation of High-Resolution 3D Medical Volumes
proposals.motivation: Clinical diagnosis and treatment planning increasingly rely on high-resolution 3D imaging (e.g., MRI, CT). However, deep learning models for 3D segmentation are notoriously data-hungry and prone to learning spurious correlations from limited annotations, a problem Gatidis has previously addressed in 2D. Extending robust, annotation-efficient learning to the full 3D domain is critical for developing reliable clinical tools that generalize across scanners and pathologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a framework combining 3D self-supervised contrastive pre-training with a 3D mutual information minimization (MIM) objective will significantly outperform standard supervised 3D models in data-efficient and out-of-distribution segmentation tasks by discouraging shortcut learning on volumetric features.
proposals.method: We will extend Gatidis's 2D framework by designing a 3D asymmetric encoder-decoder architecture (e.g., based on 3D UNet or nnUNet). First, we will pre-train the encoder using a 3D random-walk contrastive loss on unlabeled volumes. For the main task, we will integrate a 3D MIM loss, computing mutual information between the encoder's intermediate 3D feature maps and the input, and minimize it alongside the supervised segmentation loss. This forces the network to discard nuisance features and focus on robust anatomical representations. Training will leverage mixed-resolution strategies and gradient checkpointing to manage GPU memory for high-resolution volumes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, publicly available framework for robust 3D medical image segmentation with limited labels. It will provide empirical evidence on major 3D benchmarks (e.g., BraTS, MSD) that combating shortcut learning via MIM is even more critical in 3D than in 2D. The method aims to set a new standard for data efficiency and generalization in volumetric analysis.
proposals.novelty: While MIM and contrastive learning have been explored separately in 2D, their synergistic integration specifically for high-resolution 3D medical volumes is novel. This proposal directly translates the core principles of Gatidis's prior 2D work into the more complex and clinically relevant 3D domain, addressing unique challenges of volumetric context and memory constraints.

proposals.title: Domain-Agnostic MRI Analysis via Multi-Modal Self-Supervision and Mutual Information Minimization
proposals.motivation: Deep learning models for medical image analysis often fail to generalize across MRI data from different manufacturers and acquisition protocols, a major barrier to clinical deployment. This domain shift leads to performance degradation, requiring costly re-annotation and re-training for each new site. Addressing this is critical for developing robust, widely applicable AI tools in radiology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model trained with a combination of contrastive self-supervision on paired imaging-text data and explicit minimization of scanner-specific shortcut features will learn more invariant anatomical representations, leading to superior generalization across unseen MRI domains for downstream segmentation tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, a vision encoder will be pre-trained using a self-supervised contrastive loss (SimCLR) on multi-institutional MRI scans, augmented with heavy, realistic protocol-specific corruptions. Concurrently, we will align image features with paired radiology report embeddings from a frozen clinical LLM (e.g., fine-tuned ClinicalBERT) using a contrastive objective. In the second stage, for segmentation fine-tuning with limited annotations, we will add a domain-classification adversarial branch and apply Mutual Information Minimization (MIM) between the main segmentation features and the domain-branch features to explicitly discourage reliance on scanner-specific shortcuts.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, end-to-end trainable framework for scanner-agnostic medical image analysis that synergizes multi-modal self-supervision with explicit shortcut de-biasing. It will provide empirical evidence on public multi-scanner MRI datasets (e.g., MS-CMRSeg) that such an approach outperforms standard augmentation and domain-adversarial training. The released model and code will facilitate more reliable AI application in heterogeneous clinical environments.
proposals.novelty: While domain adaptation is well-studied, the novel integration of radiology text embeddings via LLMs as a semantic anchor for invariant feature learning, combined with explicit MIM-based shortcut removal during segmentation fine-tuning, is unexplored for cross-scanner generalization in MRI.

proposals.title: Physics-Constrained Deep Learning for Robust MRI Artifact Correction via Mutual Information Minimization
proposals.motivation: Deep learning models for MRI artifact correction (e.g., motion, bias field) often learn spurious correlations and non-physical shortcuts, leading to failures on out-of-distribution data and reduced clinical trust. While Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) offer a framework for incorporating domain knowledge, their application as hard constraints within complex, high-dimensional medical image architectures remains underexplored. This gap limits the physical plausibility and generalizability of automated corrections in patient imaging.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating fundamental MRI physics models (e.g., the signal equation for spin-echo or gradient-echo sequences) as hard architectural constraints will enforce physically plausible solutions, and coupling this with mutual information minimization will prevent the network from relying on non-causal, dataset-specific shortcuts, thereby improving robustness and generalization.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid encoder-decoder network where the decoder is explicitly constrained by a differentiable MRI forward model, transforming latent features into k-space or image domain predictions that inherently satisfy the physics. This constraint will be enforced via a projection layer or a Lagrangian multiplier loss term. To combat shortcut learning, we will integrate a Mutual Information Minimization (MIM) regularization term, as in Gatidis's prior work, between the learned latent representations and potential confounding factors (e.g., scanner type, protocol tags) extracted from DICOM headers. Training will use a combination of supervised loss on corrected images, the physics-constraint loss, and the MIM penalty.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, physically-grounded framework for MRI correction that is more interpretable and robust than purely data-driven models. It will provide a methodological blueprint for embedding hard physical constraints into deep learning for medical imaging, directly addressing the shortcut-learning problem to improve model reliability across diverse patient cohorts and imaging protocols.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the synergistic integration of hard PINN-style MRI physics constraints with mutual information minimization for deconfounding, moving beyond soft regularization or post-processing. This directly combines Gatidis's expertise in preventing deep learning shortcuts with a rigorous physics-based architecture, a combination not yet explored for general artifact correction tasks.

proposals.title: Self-Supervised Motion Artifact Characterization in Medical Imaging via Contrastive Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Patient motion during image acquisition is a major source of artifacts in MRI and CT, degrading diagnostic quality and often necessitating repeat scans. Current detection methods rely on comparing corrupted images to clean references or external sensor data, which are unavailable in clinical practice. A method to directly characterize motion from a single corrupted image would enable real-time quality assessment and adaptive acquisition protocols.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct types and severities of patient motion create unique, learnable signatures in the latent representation of a corrupted image, enabling a neural network to classify motion artifacts without a clean reference.
proposals.method: We will train a convolutional neural network (CNN) using a self-supervised contrastive learning framework, inspired by recent work on limited-annotation segmentation. The model will be trained on synthetic motion-corrupted medical images (e.g., from MRI motion simulation toolkits) where motion type (translation, rotation) and severity are known. To prevent the network from learning simplistic shortcuts based on global intensity, we will incorporate a mutual information minimization penalty between the artifact classification and low-level image statistics, adapting concepts from Gatidis's prior work on avoiding shortcut learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel tool for automated, reference-free motion artifact assessment, directly applicable to quality control in clinical imaging pipelines. It will also advance the understanding of how artifact signatures are encoded in image data, offering a 'dual' perspective to traditional motion correction tasks.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in framing motion artifact detection as a direct classification problem from a single image, eschewing the need for a reference or multi-channel data. It uniquely combines contrastive self-supervision for robust feature learning with mutual information minimization to ensure the model learns the intrinsic artifact signature.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Phenotype Discovery and Supervised Classification of Thermoneutral Brown Adipose Tissue from Fused PET, CMR, and Thermography Imaging
proposals.motivation: Current diagnostic assessment of metabolically active brown adipose tissue (BAT) relies heavily on FDG-PET/CT under cold exposure, which is impractical for routine screening and misses chronic or thermoneutral states. Thermography offers a non-invasive, low-cost alternative but lacks validated diagnostic models. There is a critical need for automated, integrated diagnostic tools that can decode the complex, multimodal signatures of BAT under everyday conditions to improve metabolic disease stratification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that unsupervised machine learning applied to fused PET, cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR), and thermography data will reveal novel, clinically relevant BAT phenotypes defined by distinct thermal and tissue characteristic signatures under thermoneutral conditions, and that these discovered phenotypes will serve as superior features for a supervised classifier to automate diagnostic categorization of metabolic states.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-stage multimodal pipeline. First, for unsupervised phenotype discovery, we will use a self-supervised contrastive learning framework (inspired by Gatidis' prior work on random walks for segmentation) on coregistered image patches to learn a shared embedding space, followed by clustering (e.g., HDBSCAN) to identify novel BAT phenotypes. To avoid shortcut learning from modality-specific artifacts, we will integrate mutual information minimization techniques between raw inputs and latent representations. Second, the discovered phenotype labels will be used to engineer features for training a supervised classifier (e.g., a multimodal transformer or late-fusion network) to predict integrated clinical diagnoses (active vs. chronic conditions).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel, data-driven taxonomy of thermoneutral BAT phenotypes, moving beyond cold-activated paradigms; 2) a closed-loop, automated diagnostic classifier that leverages discovered phenotypes for improved accuracy; and 3) a generalizable multimodal fusion framework that combines unsupervised discovery with supervised learning for medical imaging.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the closed-loop integration of unsupervised phenotype discovery directly into a supervised diagnostic classifier using multimodal thermal and metabolic imaging, a paradigm shift from using predefined, PET-only metrics. This approach explicitly uses ML not just for classification but for hypothesis generation about novel biological states.

ideas.description: This system develops a closed-loop MRI scanning platform that dynamically personalizes the exam in real-time to maximize diagnostic yield for rapid triage. Instead of a fixed truncated protocol, it begins with an ultra-fast multi-contrast scout, which is instantly analyzed by an embedded deep learning model. The model identifies suspected pathology and anatomical landmarks, then instructs the scanner to execute a patient-specific, optimized sequence pathway that prioritizes the most critical diagnostic information. This approach maintains speed while robustly adapting to a wide range of clinical presentations, ensuring key findings are not missed due to protocol rigidity.

ideas.title: Multi-Tracer, Multi-Modal Kinetic Modeling with Bayesian Model Selection for Specific Quantification of Myocardial Inflammation
ideas.description: This adversarially improved approach uses a dual-tracer dynamic PET protocol to separate general metabolic activity from specific inflammatory signals, employing tracers like FDG and a CXCR4-targeting agent. A Bayesian model selection framework is applied to dynamic data on a voxel-wise basis to automatically identify the most appropriate kinetic model, reducing bias from model misspecification. The resulting quantitative parametric maps of inflammation rate and specific receptor density are then coregistered and fused with cardiac MRI sequences (T2 mapping, LGE) in a hybrid PET/MRI system. A machine learning classifier integrates these multi-modal features to generate a specific diagnosis and activity score, significantly improving specificity over FDG-only methods.

ideas.title: Dynamic Multi-Modal Functional Integration for Precision Myocardial Targeting
ideas.description: This system moves beyond static hybrid imaging to create a dynamic, intra-procedural targeting platform for biopsy or ablation. It continuously integrates live electrophysiological data from high-density mapping, mechanical strain from intracardiac echocardiography, and the prior metabolic/anatomical data from PET/MRI. A validated machine learning algorithm fuses these streams to identify and highlight target regions based on a real-time functional signature of pathology. This approach directly addresses the static nature of pre-procedural imaging by ensuring targeting is guided by the dynamic functional state of the tissue during the procedure itself.

ideas.title: Federated Harmonization and Adaptive Learning for Robust Biobank-Scale Phenotype Classification
ideas.description: This project establishes a systematic framework for applying hybrid imaging classifiers across multiple, heterogeneous population biobanks. It first employs advanced harmonization techniques, such as ComBat-GAM, to remove non-biological technical variation from the imaging data across different sites and scanners. A federated learning system then deploys and iteratively refines the initial hybrid model across participating cohorts, adapting to local population characteristics while preserving data privacy. This approach ensures the classifier's performance is robust, generalizable, and free from spurious biases introduced by cohort-specific acquisition protocols, transforming a simple retrospective application into a validated, population-ready analysis pipeline.

ideas.title: A Physiology-Informed Radiomic Framework for Cardiac PET and CMR: Domain-Specific Feature Discovery and Validation
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a direct translation of oncology radiomics by developing a first-principles, cardiovascular-specific radiomic framework. It focuses on discovering and validating novel texture features from PET and CMR maps that are intrinsically linked to underlying myocardial biology and pathophysiology, such as inflammation, fibrosis, and microvascular dysfunction. The approach employs a rigorous, multi-stage pipeline that prioritizes physiological interpretability and technical robustness across imaging platforms and protocols. The final goal is to generate a new class of explainable imaging biomarkers that capture subtle disease patterns with clear biological meaning and proven clinical prognostic value.

ideas.title: Interpretable, Evidence-Attributing Network for Multi-Modal Active vs. Chronic Classification
ideas.description: This system trains a neural network on fused PET/CMR data to not only classify cardiac states but also to produce a spatially aligned, multi-modal attribution map highlighting the decisive image regions. It uses a dedicated attribution module constrained by anatomical segmentation to ensure explanations are grounded in recognizable cardiac structures. The model's final output includes both the classification and a succinct report citing the type, location, and intensity of evidence (e.g., 'focal FDG uptake in inferolateral wall matched to regional edema on CMR'), providing a transparent audit trail for the clinician.

ideas.title: Metabolic Standardization for Pathognomonic Imaging Signatures in Inflammatory Cardiomyopathy
ideas.description: This research synthesizes robust dietary preparation protocols with a hybrid imaging framework to establish a novel, quantitative diagnostic paradigm. By ensuring consistent and profound myocardial glucose suppression across all patients, the inherent metabolic signatures of cardiac sarcoidosis versus other inflammatory cardiomyopathies (e.g., myocarditis) are unmasked and can be reliably quantified. This allows for the development of machine-learning models trained on standardized metabolic and perfusion patterns to generate objective, probabilistic diagnoses. The result is a move beyond simple 'hot spot' detection toward a reproducible imaging biomarker system that improves diagnostic accuracy and enables earlier, more specific therapeutic intervention.

ideas.title: Adapting Super-Resolution Techniques from Satellite Imaging to Validate 1.5T CMR Classification
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that 3T CMR is necessary for accurate tissue mapping by adapting a solution from remote sensing. In satellite imaging, hybrid models fuse abundant low-resolution data with scarce high-resolution data to create usable, enhanced imagery. By training a super-resolution or feature-enhancement model on paired 1.5T and 3T CMR scans, we can generate 'synthetic high-quality' images from standard 1.5T inputs. The core research question becomes whether hybrid classification algorithms, when applied to these enhanced 1.5T images, yield tissue maps comparable to those from native 3T scans, potentially validating 1.5T as a sufficient and more accessible platform for clinical tissue characterization.

ideas.title: Multimodal ML to Decode Thermoneutral BAT Phenotypes for Automated Diagnostic Classification
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the hypothesis that thermography reveals metabolically distinct brown adipose tissue (BAT) states under thermoneutral conditions with machine learning techniques for automated classification. The core synergy is using unsupervised or semi-supervised ML on fused PET, cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR), and thermography data not just to classify known states, but to first identify and define novel BAT phenotypes based on thermal signatures and tissue characteristics. The discovered thermoneutral BAT phenotypes then become engineered features for a supervised classifier trained on the multimodal dataset, automating the integrated diagnosis of active versus chronic conditions. This creates a closed-loop system where machine learning both discovers new biologically relevant states and immediately leverages them to improve diagnostic accuracy beyond what is possible with predefined PET-based metrics alone.

ideas.title: Leveraging Ecological Niche Modeling to Link Hybrid Imaging Phenotypes to Clinical Trajectories
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from ecological niche modeling (ENM) in conservation biology to the problem of linking hybrid imaging phenotypes to longitudinal clinical outcomes in cardiology. In ENM, complex, multi-source environmental data is used to model and predict species' long-term survival and distribution. Analogously, we propose treating multi-parametric hybrid imaging (PET/MRI) data as a complex 'environment' for cardiac tissue. By applying machine learning algorithms similar to those used in ENM (e.g., maximum entropy or ensemble models), the research would identify critical combinations and thresholds of imaging biomarkers—such as patterns of fibrosis, inflammation, and metabolic activity—that collectively define a high-risk 'phenotypic niche.' This model would then be rigorously validated for its ability to predict long-term patient-centered outcomes like heart failure hospitalization or arrhythmic events, moving beyond single-biomarker associations to a systems-based prognostic framework.

ideas.title: Multimodal Image Fusion for Precision Targeting in Cardiac Interventions
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the solution from precision agriculture—where hybrid data layers are fused to create targeted management zones for sampling or treatment—to the problem of guiding endomyocardial biopsy or substrate ablation. In the analogous field, satellite imagery, soil conductivity, and yield data are integrated via GIS to direct variable-rate interventions. Adapted to cardiology, the solution involves creating a unified 3D cardiac map by fusing data from multiple imaging modalities (e.g., MRI, PET, electro-anatomic mapping). This fused map delineates specific 'pathology zones' such as scar border zones or active inflammatory regions. The composite model is then used intra-procedurally within the navigation system to provide real-time, spatially precise targeting for biopsy or ablation, ensuring the intervention is delivered exactly to the tissue of interest identified by the hybrid imaging signature.

ideas.title: Quantifying Myocardial Inflammation: Validating Simultaneous PET/MR Kinetic Modeling Against Software-Fused Separate Scans
ideas.description: This research direction directly compares simultaneous PET/MR against software-fused separate PET and MR scans, not for standard anatomical co-registration, but specifically for the task of dynamic FDG kinetic modeling in the myocardium. It investigates whether the inherent temporal synchronization and perfect spatial alignment of a hybrid scanner are critical for deriving accurate inflammation rate constants, or if software fusion of sequentially acquired scans is sufficient. The study will quantify the error introduced by temporal mismatch and imperfect alignment in the fused data on key kinetic parameters like the influx rate constant (Ki). This establishes a new benchmark for the diagnostic equivalence of scanner types, moving beyond qualitative image fusion to validate quantitative, model-based biomarker generation.

ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from remote sensing and environmental monitoring, where multi-sensor fusion networks are used to classify complex states like active wildfires versus burn scars by integrating disparate data types (e.g., satellite imagery, LiDAR). The adapted research idea is to use a similar multi-branch neural network architecture for integrated cardiac diagnosis. The model would have separate feature extraction branches for fused PET and CMR data, treating them as distinct but complementary 'sensors.' A fusion network would then integrate the extracted spatial, functional, and metabolic features to train a classifier that automates the active vs. chronic classification of cardiac sarcoidosis, improving upon single-modality analysis.

ideas.title: Multimodal Spectral Fingerprinting for Inflammatory Cardiomyopathy Classification
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from geology/mineralogy, where multimodal spectral fingerprinting (XRD, Raman, IR) distinguishes visually similar minerals, to solve the analogous clinical problem of differentiating cardiac sarcoidosis (CS) from myocarditis. The analogous solution is adapted by creating a diagnostic framework that fuses data from three complementary imaging 'spectra': 1) metabolic activity (FDG-PET), 2) tissue characterization (cMRI parametric mapping), and 3) specific inflammatory cell activity (targeted tracers). A machine learning model would integrate these multimodal quantitative features to identify a unique composite biomarker fingerprint for each disease, significantly improving diagnostic specificity over any single or dual-modality approach.

ideas.description: This study directly confronts the domain shift problem when translating a 3T-derived hybrid tissue classification model to 1.5T CMR. Using a paired multi-scanner dataset, it first quantifies the performance degradation on native 1.5T data. It then rigorously evaluates several domain adaptation methodologies to bridge the scanner gap, determining the most effective technique for creating a robust 1.5T model. The final output is a validated, scanner-adapted classification system that delivers reliable tissue characterization for widespread 1.5T clinical deployment.

ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics, where ML models identify subtle predictive patterns in noisy seismic waveforms for event detection. The analogous solution uses deep learning to extract complex spatiotemporal features from waveform data. Adapted to BAT research, we treat dynamic thermographic image sequences (infrared video) as spatiotemporal 'heat-flow waveforms.' Instead of analyzing mean temperature from static images, we apply 3D convolutional neural networks to learn the distinctive, multi-scale patterns of temperature change and heat dissipation over time that are the signature of brown adipose tissue activation, moving beyond simple thermal thresholds to a pattern-recognition paradigm.

ideas.title: Rapid Triage via Diagnostic Biomarker Panel
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from clinical microbiology's shift from full culture to rapid multiplex PCR panels, this idea adapts the concept of a targeted biomarker panel for medical imaging triage. The analogous solution involved identifying a limited set of the most clinically urgent pathogens and resistance genes to test for, drastically reducing turnaround time. Adapted to neuroimaging, this means developing a truncated hybrid MRI scan that focuses only on acquiring the key sequences (e.g., specific T1, T2, FLAIR, DWI, or angiographic contrasts) necessary to detect a pre-defined, shortlist of critical, triage-worthy conditions. The protocol and analysis pipeline are simplified to provide a rapid 'yes/no' output for the presence of these urgent findings, enabling faster patient prioritization and initial decision-making before a comprehensive diagnostic workup is performed.

ideas.title: Causal ML for BAT Identification via Multi-Modal Perturbation Imaging
ideas.description: This system employs a causal machine learning framework that requires paired thermographic and anatomical imaging (e.g., MRI) before and after a standardized cold stimulus. The model is trained to predict the post-stimulus thermographic map using the pre-stimulus data and the anatomical context, but its objective is penalized unless it identifies regions where the predicted thermal change aligns with known BAT physiology and is localized to anatomically plausible depots. This approach ensures the model learns the causal effect of the stimulus on heat production, rather than exploiting static anatomical or instrumental artifacts.

ideas.title: Apply geostatistical texture analysis from mineralogy to cardiac imaging
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geology/mineralogy, where geostatistical variogram analysis is used to uncover subtle spatial patterns in mineral distribution from 2D thin-section images. The analogous solution quantifies texture, grain size, and anisotropy through spatial autocorrelation functions. Adapted to cardiac imaging, this method would apply variogram analysis to PET and CMR texture maps to characterize myocardial tissue heterogeneity. By calculating spatial autocorrelation metrics across cardiac images, researchers can identify and quantify subtle, organized disease patterns that conventional radiomic features might miss, potentially revealing early biomarkers for cardiac conditions through their distinct spatial signature in imaging data.

ideas.title: Causal inference of hybrid imaging phenotypes on clinical outcomes using target trial emulation
ideas.description: This research system moves beyond correlation to establish causality by embedding hybrid imaging data within a causal inference framework. It designs and emulates target trials using longitudinal clinical registry data, treating specific imaging phenotypes as time-varying exposures. The system leverages instrumental variables and advanced methods like marginal structural models to isolate the causal effect of imaging findings on arrhythmias and heart failure, adjusting for time-dependent confounding. This approach explicitly tests whether modifying a phenotype would change outcome risk, providing robust evidence to guide targeted imaging-based interventions.

ideas.title: Correlate dynamic BAT imaging phenotypes with cardiovascular outcomes using longitudinal thermography
ideas.description: This research direction fuses longitudinal thermography tracking of supraclavicular skin temperature with hybrid imaging (e.g., PET-MRI) to establish validated imaging phenotypes of BAT activation and volume. By correlating these detailed, but infrequent, imaging snapshots with frequent, low-cost thermography data collected before and after metabolic interventions, we can derive continuous, thermography-based biomarkers that reliably reflect BAT physiology. These validated dynamic biomarkers are then linked to long-term clinical electronic health record data to determine their predictive power for the development of arrhythmias, heart failure, or other cardiometabolic outcomes, creating a practical tool for risk stratification and intervention monitoring.

ideas.title: Rapid Multispectral BAT Triage Scan (RMBT-Scan)
ideas.description: This fused idea develops a rapid, truncated imaging protocol that integrates a limited set of key anatomical MRI sequences with concurrent, co-registered multispectral optical imaging. The protocol is designed for quick subject triage, capturing essential structural data alongside hyperspectral-derived maps of tissue oxygenation and blood flow in supraclavicular and paraspinal regions. This allows for the immediate functional assessment of putative BAT depots, distinguishing between active, recruitable, and inactive states based on their combined metabolic and hemodynamic profile. The result is a sub-10-minute scan that provides a comprehensive physiological snapshot, accelerating subject stratification for metabolic studies or therapeutic interventions.

ideas.title: Model-Informed Fusion of Dynamic Thermography and Perfusion Ultrasound for Subsurface BAT Localization
ideas.description: This adversarially improved system pairs high-frame-rate infrared thermography with quantitative dynamic contrast-enhanced ultrasound (DCE-US) in a tightly synchronized setup. The DCE-US provides real-time, 3D maps of tissue vascularity and perfusion, which serve as critical priors for a computational thermal model. This model iteratively refines the estimation of subsurface heat sources, effectively localizing active BAT depots by reconciling the deep perfusion data with the dynamic surface thermal patterns. The output is a co-registered, quantitative map of likely BAT activity and perfusion, overcoming the core limitation of simple side-by-side image fusion.

ideas.title: Adapt Pharmacokinetic Compartment Modeling from Drug Metabolism to Quantify Myocardial Inflammation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from pharmacokinetics, where compartmental modeling is used to quantify drug metabolism rates, to the problem of quantifying myocardial inflammation. The analogous solution uses tracer kinetics and multi-compartment models to derive precise rate constants for distribution and metabolism. Adapted to cardiac imaging, a formal multi-compartment kinetic model (e.g., a 3-compartment model for blood, interstitial space, and phosphorylated FDG in inflammatory cells) is applied to dynamic FDG-PET data of the heart. By fitting the time-activity curves, the model will output the rate constant for FDG influx (K_i) or the phosphorylation rate (k3), which serves as a direct, quantitative measure of the myocardial tissue's inflammatory metabolic activity, moving beyond simple standardized uptake value (SUV) metrics.

ideas.title: Adapt Seismic Waveform Inversion for Thermal Source Reconstruction
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to a parallel inverse problem in geophysics—seismic waveform inversion for subsurface source reconstruction—to the challenge of isolating brown adipose tissue (BAT) signal. In seismology, the characteristics of an underground earthquake source are determined by iteratively matching simulated seismic waveforms to observed data. Adapted for thermal tomography, this involves using an array of surface temperature measurements (from active thermal imaging) as input to an inverse solver based on the Pennes bioheat equation. The algorithm would iteratively adjust the parameters (location, depth, power) of simulated subsurface metabolic heat sources within a 3D tissue model until the predicted surface thermal map converges with the experimental data, effectively performing a 'thermal waveform inversion' to reconstruct and isolate the BAT signal from overlying fat and other tissues.

ideas.title: Vascular Thermography-Guided Biopsy and Ablation of Cardiac Sympathetic Targets
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using high-resolution thermal imaging to map cutaneous vascular changes overlying key sympathetic ganglia (e.g., stellate ganglion) and cardiac substrate, correlating these thermal signatures with hybrid PET/MR or PET/CT imaging of neuronal or metabolic activity. The core innovation is using dynamic thermography as a real-time, non-invasive surrogate for localized sympathetic tone or inflammatory metabolic activity to plan procedures. This enables targeted biopsy of peri-ganglionic fat or ablation of specific cardiac substrate regions identified by a combination of anatomical hybrid imaging and functional thermal signaling, potentially improving precision and reducing procedural morbidity.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Signal Enhancement Techniques for BAT Detection in Low-Noise Anatomies
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from geophysics, specifically the challenge of detecting subtle seismic signals in tectonically quiet, low-noise regions. The analogous solution involves using synchronized sensor arrays and targeted signal processing to amplify weak, known signal patterns. Adapted to BAT research, the study would investigate infrared thermography for BAT detection in younger, leaner populations by treating their anatomy as a 'low-noise' environment where the BAT thermal signal is faint. The research would develop and validate a novel methodology employing synchronized, multi-perspective high-resolution IR imaging and advanced computational analysis (e.g., pattern recognition algorithms tuned for expected BAT depot morphology and activation kinetics) to significantly improve the sensitivity and specificity of non-invasive BAT detection in individuals with low BMI and subcutaneous fat thickness.

ideas.title: Adapt Phase-Contrast Analysis from Volcanology to Map BAT Perfusion Dynamics
ideas.description: The original idea proposed using high-speed thermal imaging to capture rapid temperature fluctuations in brown adipose tissue (BAT). By drawing an analogy from volcanology, where scientists use phase-correlation analysis on thermal video to track the motion and spreading of heat in chaotic lava flows, we can adapt a sophisticated solution. The adapted research idea is to apply these phase-correlation and optical flow algorithms to dynamic BAT thermography. This would transform raw thermal fluctuations into a quantifiable map of perfusion dynamics, revealing the patterns and velocities of vascular control events that are currently difficult to resolve amidst biological noise.

ideas.title: Adapting Agricultural Pre-Harvest Protocols for Standardized Myocardial Glucose Suppression
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from precision agriculture, where pre-harvest protocols are meticulously designed to produce uniform crop physiology. The analogous solution involves multi-variable control systems in controlled environment horticulture that adjust light, nutrients, and conditioning treatments to ensure consistent metabolic outcomes. Adapted to cardiac PET imaging, the research idea is to develop a robust, closed-loop preparation protocol. This system would use continuous biomarker monitoring (glucose, ketones, hormones) via wearables and an algorithm to guide personalized, timed interventions—such as micro-dosed nutritional cues, prescribed activity, or mild physiological conditioning—in the hours leading up to the scan. The goal is to actively drive all patients, despite individual metabolic differences, into a consistently suppressed myocardial glucose utilization state, thereby standardizing the biological baseline for imaging and improving diagnostic accuracy.

ideas.title: Multi-Sensor Array for BAT Thermal Mapping
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that the supraclavicular fossa is the sole best site for BAT thermal signal by drawing an analogy from geophysical mineral exploration. In that field, the solution to finding the best signal for subsurface deposits is not to find a single best sensor location but to deploy a distributed array of sensors and fuse the data to create a superior composite map. Adapted to BAT research, this suggests developing a wearable, high-resolution thermal sensor array to simultaneously map temperature profiles across the supraclavicular, paravertebral, mediastinal, and perirenal regions. The research would use data fusion algorithms to integrate these multi-point readings, testing whether a composite 'thermal signature' from several regions provides a stronger correlation with BAT activity (e.g., via PET-CT confirmation) than the supraclavicular signal alone, thereby accounting for individual anatomical and physiological variation in BAT distribution.

ideas.title: Metabolic Inertia Following Sequential Cold-Warm Challenge as a Specific Biomarker for BAT Activity
ideas.description: This adversarially improved protocol challenges the thermoneutral baseline by using a dynamic thermal sequence to isolate BAT-specific signatures. It first uses a brief, standardized cold exposure to activate any functional BAT, then applies a mild warm stimulus while precisely measuring the metabolic rate. The key novel metric is 'metabolic inertia'—the delayed return to baseline energy expenditure—which is hypothesized to be directly proportional to the amount and activity of activated BAT. This approach bypasses the confounding effects of passive vasodilation by focusing on the dissipation kinetics of active thermogenesis, providing a more specific and quantifiable functional readout than a single thermal state.
